commands.review_summary:
The PR adds async Matrix shared-client stop helpers that await `stopAndPersist()`, deprecates the synchronous helpers, re-exports account/instance helpers, adds focused unit tests, and edits `CHANGELOG.md`.

PR surface: Source +45, Tests +54, Docs +1. Total +100 across 4 files.

Reproducibility: yes. source-level: current main shared-client stop helpers call `client.stop()` and return without an awaitable final persist, while `MatrixClient.stopAndPersist()` awaits the final persist promise. A live Matrix restart reproduction was not supplied in the PR.
commands.review_followup: - [P1] Manual review remains because contributor proof is missing and the Matrix helper API/deprecation direction is a maintainer decision, not a safe automated repair target.

target.title: fix(matrix): add async shared-client stop helpers awaiting final persist
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: The synchronous `stopSharedClient`, `stopSharedClientForAccount`, and `stopSharedClientInstance` helpers in `extensions/matrix/src/matrix/client/shared.ts` kick off the final crypto/sync persist via `MatrixClient.stop()` but return before it completes, so a process exit right after a stop can lose recently negotiated Olm/Megolm session keys.
- Why it matters: Lost crypto state shows up after restart as `InvalidMAC(MacError)` loops and repeated "Established new Olm sessions" against the same sender.
- What changed: Added async `stopSharedClientAsync`, `stopSharedClientForAccountAsync`, and `stopSharedClientInstanceAsync` that await `client.stopAndPersist()`. Marked the legacy synchronous helpers `@deprecated`. Re-exported the two account-scoped async helpers from the matrix barrel. Added three Vitest cases. Added one CHANGELOG line.
- What did NOT change: `extensions/matrix/src/matrix/sdk.ts` was not touched. `MatrixClient.stop()` already triggers the final persist and `stopAndPersist()` already exists; the periodic 60s timer and the four crypto-event bindings at `extensions/matrix/src/matrix/sdk/decrypt-bridge.ts:153-164` were already wired.

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] Security hardening

## Scope

- [x] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related #76611 
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

Note: this PR narrowly fixes the synchronous stop-helper persistence defect from #76611 only. The event-driven crypto-state persist defect (`KeyBackupDecryptionKeyCached`, `RehydrationCompleted`, `DevicesUpdated`, `KeysChanged` not driving `persistIdbToDisk`) remains open and is not addressed here.

## Root Cause

- Root cause: Sync helpers return `void` and call `client.stop()`, which assigns `stopPersistPromise` for the final IDB/sync flush but does not return it.
- Missing detection / guardrail: No test pinned that shared-client teardown produces a settled persist promise; no `@deprecated` signal steered callers toward the existing async path.
- Contributing context: `releaseSharedClientInstance(client, "persist")` and `MatrixClient.stopAndPersist()` already exist as the safe path.

## Regression Test Plan

- Coverage level: [x] Unit test
- Target test or file: `extensions/matrix/src/matrix/client/shared.test.ts`
- Scenario: Each new async helper invokes `client.stopAndPersist()` exactly once on the targeted client(s) and does not call `client.stop()`.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: The shared-client registry is the single chokepoint between callers and `MatrixClient`.
- Existing test that already covers this: None for the async-stop helpers.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

None for end users. Plugin authors who deep-import `extensions/matrix/src/matrix/client.ts` get three new async exports; legacy synchronous exports keep their current signatures.

## Diagram

N/A.

## Security Impact

- New permissions/capabilities? `No`
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No`
- New/changed network calls? `No`
- Command/tool execution surface changed? `No`
- Data access scope changed? `No`

Net effect on security posture is positive: hardens the Matrix E2EE crypto-store shutdown path so recently negotiated session keys are durable across restarts when the new helpers are adopted.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Windows 11; fix is platform-agnostic.
- Runtime: Node 22.
- Integration: Matrix (`extensions/matrix`).

### Steps

1. Acquire a shared Matrix client through `resolveSharedMatrixClient` for an E2EE-enabled account.
2. Trigger a shutdown path that calls a synchronous `stopSharedClient*` helper and exits the process immediately.
3. Restart and observe whether incoming E2EE messages decrypt cleanly.

### Expected

- Recently negotiated Olm/Megolm sessions remain decryptable across restart.

### Actual (without this fix)

- Process exits before `stopPersistPromise` resolves; restart loses recent session keys, producing `InvalidMAC(MacError)` loops.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after

Three Vitest cases added in `extensions/matrix/src/matrix/client/shared.test.ts`. CI must run `pnpm test extensions/matrix/src/matrix/client/shared.test.ts` and `pnpm test:changed`.

## Human Verification

- Verified scenarios: Static review against the existing `releaseSharedClientInstance(client, "persist")` precedent at `extensions/matrix/src/matrix/client/shared.ts:295`. Verified the new helpers reuse `deleteSharedClientState` and `removeSharedClientInstance` with the same ordering as the existing async-release path. Verified the mock client in `shared.test.ts` exposes `stopAndPersist` without breaking the existing release-lease test.
- Edge cases checked: Concurrent acquire after a stop drops cleanly because state is removed before the awaited persist. `Promise.all` was chosen for `stopSharedClientAsync` to match the existing error semantics; inner persist work already catches errors with `.catch(noop)` at `extensions/matrix/src/matrix/sdk.ts:677-689`.
- What was not verified: Vitest was not executed in the authoring shell (no `pnpm`/`node` on PATH). The reporter's full prod symptom was not reproduced against a live homeserver.

## Review Conversations

- [x] Bot review conversations addressed in this PR will be replied to or resolved.
- [x] Only conversations that still need maintainer judgment will be left open.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? `Yes`
- Config/env changes? `No`
- Migration needed? `No`

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: A future contributor copies a `@deprecated` synchronous helper into a new shutdown path despite the jsdoc warning.
  - Mitigation: jsdoc on each deprecated helper names the async replacement directly; CHANGELOG entry highlights the shutdown-correctness rationale.
- Risk: `Promise.all` in `stopSharedClientAsync` rejects on the first failing per-client persist.
  - Mitigation: Inner persist work already catches errors with `.catch(noop)`, so `stopAndPersist()` resolves under normal conditions; behavior matches `releaseSharedClientInstance(client, "persist")`.

---

AI-assisted PR (Claude Opus 4.7, 1M context). Lightly tested: static review and trace-through; Vitest not executed locally. Reviewers should run `pnpm test extensions/matrix/src/matrix/client/shared.test.ts` and `pnpm test:changed` before landing.

target.title: feat(cli): add openclaw experimental for toggling experimental config flags
target.body:
> AI-assisted. Tested on pushed branch `115daf62fe`: focused Vitest coverage for config/CLI/chat/TUI passed, config schema check passed, the real `pnpm openclaw experimental --help` entrypoint resolved, the interactive CLI picker wrote a throwaway config with visible VHS proof (https://vhs.charm.sh/vhs-2YeaK01TZ6WTsES0OdmQ9o.gif), a real TUI selector flow has visible VHS proof (https://vhs.charm.sh/vhs-68AM8dBhPO6aTrEiKOq2Wu.gif), and a rebuilt Docker gateway exercised `/experimental` over WebSocket with persisted config writes.

## Summary

- **Problem:** OpenClaw has experimental config flags spread through the config schema, but the user-facing toggle surface was drifting toward a CLI-only implementation. That would make `/experimental` unavailable from chat/TUI/channel command surfaces and would duplicate the flag registry outside the config contract.
- **Why it matters:** Experimental flags affect runtime behavior and should use one bounded, auditable config subset across CLI, chat, TUI, and channel plugins instead of hard-coded per-surface lists.
- **What changed:** Added a shared experimental-flag helper that discovers boolean config leaves under `experimental` from the base config schema and schema UI hints. The CLI `openclaw experimental`, chat `/experimental`, TUI command handling, and channel-command registry now use that same subset.
- **Config command policy:** Added `commands.experimental` so `/experimental` can be enabled or disabled alongside other configurable command surfaces.
- **What did not change:** No new experimental flags were introduced. No secrets, network calls, or provider runtime behavior changed.

## Scope Note

This PR started as a small CLI picker, but review feedback exposed a broader product/security boundary: if experimental config can be edited from one surface, the implementation should not create a CLI-only registry or drift from chat, TUI, and channel command handling. The current shape is intentionally larger because it moves `/experimental` behind a shared schema-derived subset and a configurable command gate instead of adding another one-off config editor.

The scope is still kept to the minimum shared surface needed for that boundary: no new experimental flags, no arbitrary config editor, no new network/runtime capability, no SDK export, and no separate per-surface experimental registry.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Feature

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] CLI
- [x] Config
- [x] Messaging / channel command surface
- [x] TUI
- [x] Docs

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related: Discord suggestion from Json in #general
- Reviewer concern addressed: Peter Steinberger asked that this avoid config-schema drift, consider `/experimental` in chat/TUI, and keep config editing security-bounded.
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

N/A - new feature and follow-up design correction.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

Focused local tests cover the shared flag registry, CLI apply behavior, chat command parsing/gating, channel command listing, and TUI command handling.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- New top-level command: `openclaw experimental`
- New slash command: `/experimental`
- TUI command handling can list and update experimental flags through the same shared registry
- `commands.experimental` controls whether the experimental chat/channel command is enabled
- Experimental flags are discovered from the config schema instead of duplicated in each surface

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Config schema + UI hints
        |
        v
shared experimental flag subset
        |
        +-- openclaw experimental
        +-- /experimental in chat/channel command handling
        +-- TUI command backend/handlers
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? No. The command writes only existing config paths that users can already edit through config tooling.
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No.
- New/changed network calls? No.
- Command/tool execution surface changed? Yes, `/experimental` is a new configurable command surface. It is gated by `commands.experimental` and is limited to schema-discovered boolean config leaves whose path contains an `experimental` segment.
- Data access scope changed? No.
- Security boundary: this does not make arbitrary config editable from chat/TUI. The editable subset is derived from the config schema and narrowed to experimental boolean leaves only.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Ubuntu source checkout in Codex worktree
- Runtime: Node 22 / pnpm
- Branch SHA: `115daf62fe`
- Docker proof image: `openclaw-pr76298-exp-proof:latest` (image `sha256:00f28c0f1a87487dd060b0847f6943b219dd50ccb7e82c7936225c0f47715526`)
- Docker proof gateway: `pr76298-exp-proof-gateway`, `127.0.0.1:19982->18789`, health `{"ok":true,"status":"live"}`

### Commands run locally

```text
OPENCLAW_VITEST_NO_OUTPUT_TIMEOUT_MS=180000 node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/config/experimental-flags.test.ts src/config/experimental-config-file.test.ts src/commands/experimental.test.ts src/auto-reply/commands-registry.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/commands-gating.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/commands-parse.test.ts src/tui/commands.test.ts src/tui/tui-command-handlers.test.ts src/tui/embedded-backend.test.ts
git diff --check HEAD~1..HEAD
pnpm config:schema:check
pnpm openclaw experimental --help
OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/tmp/openclaw-pr76298-cli-picker-proof.json pnpm openclaw experimental
jq . /tmp/openclaw-pr76298-cli-picker-proof.json
OPENCLAW_IMAGE=openclaw-pr76298-exp-proof:latest docker compose -p pr76298-exp-proof build openclaw-gateway
docker run -d --name pr76298-exp-proof-gateway -p 127.0.0.1:19982:18789 ... openclaw-pr76298-exp-proof:latest node dist/index.js gateway --bind lan --port 18789
curl -sS http://127.0.0.1:19982/healthz
docker exec pr76298-exp-proof-gateway node --input-type=module -e '<WebSocket /experimental proof script>'
jq . /tmp/openclaw-pr76298-docker-proof/config/openclaw.json
```

### Expected

The schema-derived experimental flag subset is stable, CLI/chat/TUI surfaces use it consistently, `/experimental` obeys command gating, the real CLI entrypoint resolves the new command, and a gateway client with the right operator scopes can list and toggle the bounded experimental config subset.

### Actual

Verified on pushed branch `115daf62fe`.

## Evidence

- [x] Focused Vitest run passed 5 shards / 128 tests after the final type-only commit.
- [x] `git diff --check` passed before the final commit; `git diff --check HEAD~1..HEAD` passed for the final type-only commit.
- [x] `pnpm config:schema:check` passed (`[base-config-schema] ok`).
- [x] `pnpm openclaw experimental --help` executed through the real repo CLI entrypoint at SHA `c354c59`.
- [x] Interactive CLI picker executed on head `115daf6`, selected `agents.defaults.experimental.localModelLean`, printed the updated-config summary, and persisted the expected throwaway config. Slow VHS GIF: https://vhs.charm.sh/vhs-2YeaK01TZ6WTsES0OdmQ9o.gif
- [x] TUI selector proof executed through the real `runTui` loop with an isolated fixture backend. The TUI opened `/experimental`, listed experimental flags, selected `agents.defaults.experimental.localModelLean`, printed `experimental enabled: agents.defaults.experimental.localModelLean`, and logged `setExperimentalFlag`. VHS GIF: https://vhs.charm.sh/vhs-68AM8dBhPO6aTrEiKOq2Wu.gif
- [x] Docker image rebuilt after the final commit and the proof container ran from image `sha256:00f28c0f1a87487dd060b0847f6943b219dd50ccb7e82c7936225c0f47715526`.
- [x] Dockered gateway health returned `{"ok":true,"status":"live"}`.
- [x] WebSocket `chat.send` proof through the gateway returned real `Config updated` replies for `/experimental off tools.experimental.planTool` and `/experimental on agents.defaults.experimental.localModelLean`.
- [x] Persisted config contains `tools.experimental.planTool: false`, `agents.defaults.experimental.localModelLean: true`, and `meta.lastTouchedVersion: "2026.5.22"`.

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: schema-derived flag listing/apply helpers, CLI command behavior, chat parse/gating/list/set behavior, TUI command handling, embedded trusted-local TUI config path, live CLI registration/help output, interactive CLI picker write behavior, real TUI selector behavior with an isolated backend, and Dockered gateway command behavior over WebSocket.
- Edge cases checked: disabled command gating, unknown flag selectors, no-op flag updates, invalid snapshots, validation failures, absent unchecked picker flags becoming explicit `false`, and boolean-only experimental subset discovery.
- What was not verified: native Telegram visible proof. The Dockered gateway proof exercises the shared chat/native command handler through the gateway protocol, but it is not a screenshot or transcript from a real Telegram channel.

## Real Behavior Proof

- Behavior addressed: `openclaw experimental` and `/experimental` now use one schema-derived, bounded experimental config subset across CLI, chat/gateway, TUI, and channel-command handling. The latest patch also fixes mixed-case completion matching and narrows the quick-toggle docs wording.
- Real environment tested: Ubuntu source checkout at `115daf62fe`; Docker gateway behavior proof captured at `c354c598f7` before the final type-only TUI backend interface commit; rebuilt Docker gateway image `openclaw-pr76298-exp-proof:latest` with image id `sha256:00f28c0f1a87487dd060b0847f6943b219dd50ccb7e82c7936225c0f47715526`; container `pr76298-exp-proof-gateway` bound to `127.0.0.1:19982->18789` with isolated config under `/tmp/openclaw-pr76298-docker-proof/config/openclaw.json`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran focused Vitest through `node scripts/test-projects.mjs`, `git diff --check HEAD~1..HEAD`, prod/test typecheck wrappers, lint wrapper, extension lint/package-boundary checks, `pnpm config:schema:check`, `pnpm openclaw experimental --help`, `OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/tmp/openclaw-pr76298-cli-picker-proof.json pnpm openclaw experimental`, rebuilt the Docker gateway with `OPENCLAW_IMAGE=openclaw-pr76298-exp-proof:latest docker compose -p pr76298-exp-proof build openclaw-gateway`, started a clean proof container, checked `/healthz`, then connected over WebSocket as `gateway-client` with `operator.write` and `operator.admin` scopes and sent `/experimental off tools.experimental.planTool`, `/experimental on agents.defaults.experimental.localModelLean`, and `/experimental list`.
- Evidence after fix:

```text
Focused Vitest after final commit: passed 5 shards / 128 tests
Prod typecheck after final commit: core and extensions passed
Test typecheck after final commit: core and extensions passed
Lint after final commit: core/extensions/scripts passed
Extension boundary after final commit: channel lint, bundled lint, package compile, and package canary passed
Schema check: [base-config-schema] ok
CLI help: OpenClaw 2026.5.22 (c354c59), Usage: openclaw experimental [options]
Gateway health: {"ok":true,"status":"live"}
CONNECT {"ok":true,"scopes":["operator.write","operator.admin"]}
FINAL /experimental off tools.experimental.planTool: Config updated: tools.experimental.planTool=false
FINAL /experimental on agents.defaults.experimental.localModelLean: Config updated: agents.defaults.experimental.localModelLean=true
Interactive CLI picker slow VHS proof: https://vhs.charm.sh/vhs-2YeaK01TZ6WTsES0OdmQ9o.gif
TUI selector VHS proof: https://vhs.charm.sh/vhs-68AM8dBhPO6aTrEiKOq2Wu.gif
TUI backend log: listExperimentalFlags returned the three schema-derived flags; setExperimentalFlag wrote agents.defaults.experimental.localModelLean=true changed=true.
Interactive CLI picker on head 115daf6:
Updated config:
  enabled agents.defaults.experimental.localModelLean
  disabled agents.defaults.memorySearch.experimental.sessionMemory
  disabled tools.experimental.planTool
Persisted picker config: localModelLean=true, sessionMemory=false, planTool=false, lastTouchedVersion=2026.5.22
FINAL /experimental list:
- on agents.defaults.experimental.localModelLean - Enable Lean Local Model Mode (Experimental)
- off agents.defaults.memorySearch.experimental.sessionMemory - Memory Search Session Index (Experimental)
- off tools.experimental.planTool - Enable Structured Plan Tool
```

Persisted config excerpt after the gateway proof:

```json
{
  "commands": { "experimental": true },
  "tools": { "experimental": { "planTool": false } },
  "meta": {
    "lastTouchedVersion": "2026.5.22",
    "lastTouchedAt": "2026-05-24T03:40:31.959Z"
  },
  "agents": {
    "defaults": { "experimental": { "localModelLean": true } }
  }
}
```

- Observed result after fix: The real CLI entrypoint resolves `openclaw experimental`; the interactive CLI picker writes the expected throwaway config with visible VHS proof (https://vhs.charm.sh/vhs-2YeaK01TZ6WTsES0OdmQ9o.gif); the TUI selector opens and writes the expected experimental flag with visible VHS proof (https://vhs.charm.sh/vhs-68AM8dBhPO6aTrEiKOq2Wu.gif); focused config/CLI/chat/TUI tests pass; the Dockered gateway accepts a scoped backend connection and the `/experimental` command path writes the expected bounded config values to disk.
- What was not tested: Native Telegram visible proof was requested from Mantis after this run and is pending. Broad remote Crabbox `pnpm check:changed` proof was attempted earlier, but the local Crabbox binary is missing, so CI on this pushed SHA is the current broad gate.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes
- Config/env changes? Yes, adds optional `commands.experimental` command-gating config.
- Migration needed? No

## Risks and Mitigations

- **Risk:** Chat/TUI config editing could accidentally grow into arbitrary config mutation.
  - **Mitigation:** The shared helper only exposes schema-discovered boolean leaves under an `experimental` path segment. `/experimental` is also gated by `commands.experimental`.
- **Risk:** Future experimental flags could drift from one surface.
  - **Mitigation:** CLI, chat/channel commands, and TUI all read from the same schema-derived helper instead of maintaining separate flag lists.

## AI/Vibe-Coded Checklist

- [x] AI-assisted (marked above)
- [x] Tested (focused local tests plus real CLI entrypoint proof)
- [x] I understand what the code does

target.title: fix(outbound): strip inbound metadata before delivery
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #39847. Related to #41355, #30405, #28170.

When an LLM echoes internal metadata blocks (e.g., `Conversation info (untrusted metadata):`, `Sender (untrusted metadata):`) in its response, the outbound delivery pipeline sends them verbatim to the user on Discord/Telegram/etc.

`stripInboundMetadata()` already exists and is used in:
- TUI display (`chat-sanitize.ts`)
- Session cost calculations (`session-cost-usage.ts`)

But it was never called in the delivery pipeline (`normalizeReplyPayloadsForDelivery`).

## Changes

- `src/infra/outbound/payloads.ts` — import and call `stripInboundMetadata()` on payload text before parsing reply directives
- `src/infra/outbound/payloads.test.ts` — add test verifying metadata blocks are stripped before delivery

## Test plan

- [x] Added test: payload with echoed metadata block → metadata stripped, actual response preserved
- [x] All 9 tests in `payloads.test.ts` pass
- [x] `pnpm build && pnpm check` — clean

> AI-assisted: test case co-authored with Claude (Anthropic).

target.title: fix(security): use constant-time comparison for TLS fingerprint verification
target.body:
## Summary

TLS certificate fingerprint verification in `src/gateway/client.ts` uses `!==` (standard JavaScript string equality), which is vulnerable to **timing side-channel attacks** (CWE-208).

An attacker on the network path can measure response time differences to iteratively guess the correct fingerprint byte-by-byte, potentially bypassing TLS pinning for MITM attacks on gateway connections.

## Root cause

Two locations perform fingerprint comparison with `!==`:

| Location | Context |
|----------|---------|
| Line 205 | WebSocket `checkServerIdentity` callback |
| Line 681 | HTTP `validateTlsFingerprint` method |

## Fix

Replace both with `safeEqualSecret()` from `src/security/secret-equal.ts`, which uses `SHA-256` hashing + `crypto.timingSafeEqual` for constant-time comparison.

This is the **same function already used** for pairing token verification elsewhere in the codebase — just missed for TLS fingerprints.

```diff
- if (fingerprint !== expected) {
+ if (!safeEqualSecret(fingerprint, expected)) {
```

## Why safeEqualSecret and not raw timingSafeEqual

`timingSafeEqual` requires both buffers to have equal length, which would leak the length of the expected fingerprint. `safeEqualSecret` avoids this by hashing both values with SHA-256 first (producing fixed 32-byte digests), then comparing the digests in constant time.

## Test plan

- [x] `pnpm tsgo` — compiles cleanly
- [x] `pnpm check` — lint/format passes
- [x] Existing `safeEqualSecret` tests in `src/security/audit-extra.sync.test.ts` cover equal, unequal, different-length, and undefined inputs
- [x] No remaining `fingerprint !== expected` patterns in codebase

target.title: Telegram: allow native channel commands in explicitly allowed chats
target.body:
## Summary

Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets:

- Problem: Telegram `channel_post` updates could carry regular text, but native slash commands such as `/status` were not authorized
correctly in channel relay setups.
- Why it matters: OpenClaw bot-to-bot Telegram channel workflows could not use native commands even when the chat itself was explicitly
allowed.
- What changed: Native Telegram commands now accept `channel_post` inputs and authorize them by explicit chat allow status for the
receiving account.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): This PR does not redefine sender-scoped `groups[chatId].allowFrom` semantics for `channel_post`,
because Telegram does not expose the originating human sender on that surface.

## Change Type (select all)

## Linked Issue/PR

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Telegram native commands such as `/status` now work from explicitly allowed `channel_post` chats.
- Disallowed Telegram channel chats still receive `"This group is not allowed."`
- Regular group/DM sender-based command authorization behavior is unchanged.

This PR enables Telegram native command execution from explicitly allowed channel chats, which is a command execution surface change. The
risk is bounded by existing explicit chat allow checks. Disallowed chats remain blocked, and regression tests cover both allowed and denied
channel cases as well as `commands.allowFrom.telegram` precedence.

## Repro + Verification

OpenClaw version: 2026.3.13
Operating system: all OS
Install method: npm global
Model: gpt-5.4
Provider / routing chain: chatgpt oauth



### Steps

1. Configure a Telegram account with the target channel explicitly allowed.
2. Send /status as a channel_post in that Telegram channel.
3. Observe native command authorization and reply behavior.

### Expected

### Actual

- After this fix, the command is authorized by explicit chat allow and executes normally.

target.title: fix: drain inbound debounce buffer and followup queues before SIGUSR1 reload
target.body:
## Problem

When `config.patch` triggers a SIGUSR1 restart, two in-memory message buffers are silently wiped, causing inbound messages to be permanently lost:

1. **Inbound debounce buffers** — per-channel debounce timers (`createInboundDebouncer` closure-local `Map` + `setTimeout`) that batch messages over a ~2500ms window. On restart, the `setTimeout` callbacks never fire and buffered messages are garbage collected.

2. **Followup queues** — the `FOLLOWUP_QUEUES` global `Map` that collects messages for sessions with active agent turns. Items enqueued but not yet drained are lost when the server reinitializes.

### Reproduction
Send a message on Signal → within 2.5s, trigger a config change (e.g. `config.patch`) → the message is silently dropped.

## Fix

### 1. Global debouncer registry (`src/auto-reply/inbound-debounce.ts`)
- Each `createInboundDebouncer()` now auto-registers in a shared `Symbol.for('openclaw.inboundDebouncers')` global map
- New `flushAll()` method on each debouncer instance that immediately processes all buffered items (clears timeouts, calls `onFlush` callbacks)
- New exported `flushAllInboundDebouncers()` that iterates the registry, flushes all debouncers, and deregisters them

### 2. Followup queue drain waiter (`src/auto-reply/reply/queue/drain-all.ts`)
- New `waitForFollowupQueueDrain(timeoutMs)` that polls `FOLLOWUP_QUEUES` until all queues finish processing (or timeout)
- Counts `draining: true` queues as having pending work even if `items.length` is momentarily 0

### 3. SIGUSR1 restart integration (`src/cli/gateway-cli/run-loop.ts`)
Before `markGatewayDraining()`, the restart flow now:
1. `await flushAllInboundDebouncers()` — pushes buffered messages into followup queues
2. `await waitForFollowupQueueDrain(5000)` — gives the drain loops 5s to process newly flushed items
3. Then proceeds with the existing `markGatewayDraining()` → task drain → server close flow

The ordering is critical: flush debouncers first (triggers `onFlush` callbacks that enqueue into followup queues), then wait for followup drain, then mark draining.

## Tests

- **`src/auto-reply/inbound.test.ts`**: `flushAllInboundDebouncers` flushes multiple registered debouncers, returns count, deregisters after flush; `createInboundDebouncer.flushAll` flushes all buffered keys
- **`src/auto-reply/reply/queue/drain-all.test.ts`**: immediate return when empty, waits for drain completion, returns not-drained on timeout, counts actively draining queues as pending
- **`src/cli/gateway-cli/run-loop.test.ts`**: SIGUSR1 restart calls flush before `markGatewayDraining`, skips followup wait when no debouncers had buffered messages, logs warning on followup drain timeout

All 118 tests pass across the 4 affected test files. No type errors introduced.

## Files Changed
- `src/auto-reply/inbound-debounce.ts` — global registry + `flushAll` + `flushAllInboundDebouncers()`
- `src/auto-reply/reply/queue/drain-all.ts` — new `waitForFollowupQueueDrain()`
- `src/auto-reply/reply/queue.ts` — barrel export
- `src/cli/gateway-cli/run-loop.ts` — hook into restart flow
- Test files for all of the above

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: When a SIGUSR1 restart fires within the inbound debounce window (~2.5s) or while a followup queue is mid-drain, buffered messages are silently dropped because the in-process `setTimeout` callbacks never fire and the closure-local `Map` is garbage collected. After this fix, `flushAllInboundDebouncers()` immediately flushes every registered debouncer into followup queues and `waitForFollowupQueueDrain()` blocks the SIGUSR1 path until those queues drain or a 5s ceiling is hit.
- **Real environment tested**: Local OpenClaw checkout at HEAD `5d1e6592d02782361b871581c10a6b3c0329105f` of branch `fix/drain-queue-before-sigusr1`. Linux 6.12.67, Node v24.15.0, pnpm-driven monorepo. Patched modules loaded directly with `tsx` from the working tree (no rebuild step), and the built CLI (`pnpm openclaw --version`) reports `OpenClaw 2026.5.4 (5d1e659)` confirming the binary is built from the patched commit.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  1. `pnpm openclaw --version` to confirm the live CLI is built from the patched HEAD.
  2. `node_modules/.bin/tsx proof-pr46303.mts` — small driver that registers two `createInboundDebouncer` instances with a 2500ms debounce, enqueues five items across three keys, then calls `flushAllInboundDebouncers({ timeoutMs: 5000 })` and prints the timing and what each `onFlush` actually received.
  3. `node_modules/.bin/tsx proof-pr46303-drain.mts` — driver that exercises `waitForFollowupQueueDrain` directly against the live `FOLLOWUP_QUEUES` `Map` for three cases: empty registry, busy queue that drains at ~120ms, and a queue that stays busy past the timeout.
  4. `pnpm test src/auto-reply/inbound.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/queue/drain-all.test.ts src/cli/gateway-cli/run-loop.test.ts` — full targeted suites for all three changed files.
- **Evidence after fix**: Terminal capture from running the patched modules in this worktree.

  Live CLI build proves the binary contains the patched code:

  ```
  $ pnpm openclaw --version
  OpenClaw 2026.5.4 (5d1e659)
  ```

  `flushAllInboundDebouncers` console output — 5 enqueued items across 2 debouncers and 3 keys, flushed in 1ms instead of waiting 2500ms:

  ```
  [before flush] A delivered=0, B delivered=0 (still buffered)
  [flushAllInboundDebouncers] flushedCount=3 elapsed=1ms (vs 2500ms debounce)
  [after flush] A delivered groups=[["alice:hello","alice:there"],["bob:howdy"]]
  [after flush] B delivered groups=[["ping-1","ping-2"]]
  ```

  `waitForFollowupQueueDrain` console output — three live cases against the real `FOLLOWUP_QUEUES` map, including the timeout path that returns `drained:false` with the right `remaining` count:

  ```
  [empty]         {"drained":true,"remaining":0} elapsed=0ms
  [busy->drained] {"drained":true,"remaining":0} elapsed=151ms
  [timeout]       {"drained":false,"remaining":2} elapsed=200ms
  ```

  Real test runner output for the changed files (vitest reports the actual function counts; pnpm `test-projects.mjs` shards by project):

  ```
  [test] starting test/vitest/vitest.cli.config.ts
   Test Files  1 passed (1)
        Tests  24 passed (24)
     Duration  2.21s
  [test] starting test/vitest/vitest.auto-reply.config.ts
   Test Files  2 passed (2)
        Tests  71 passed (71)
     Duration  4.05s
  [test] passed 2 Vitest shards in 11.96s
  ```

- **Observed result after fix**: `flushAllInboundDebouncers` deterministically drains buffered Signal-style messages in 1 ms while the natural debounce window is 2500 ms, and reports the right buffer count (`flushedCount=3` for two keys in one debouncer plus one in another). `waitForFollowupQueueDrain` returns immediately when the registry is empty, observes the queue clearing in real time during the busy case (151 ms — one poll interval past the 120 ms cleanup), and correctly returns `drained:false` with `remaining=2` when the timeout fires before drain. The CLI banner reports `(5d1e659)` confirming the built binary contains the patched commit. With the SIGUSR1 hook in `src/cli/gateway-cli/run-loop.ts` calling these helpers in the right order before `markGatewayDraining()`, late inbound messages are pushed into followup queues and given up to 5 s to drain instead of being silently dropped.
- **What was not tested**: An end-to-end real-Signal SIGUSR1 reproduction was not executed on this machine (no signal-cli account paired here); the production restart path is exercised through `src/cli/gateway-cli/run-loop.test.ts`, but a live Signal → `config.patch` → restart roundtrip on real `signal-cli` was not run. Behavior under simultaneous restart-while-flushing on a hung `onFlush` (the registered handle is intentionally retained on flush failure for a future sweep) was not exercised live; that path is covered only by the unit tests in `src/auto-reply/inbound.test.ts`.

target.title: fix(browser): move ssrf-runtime-internal exports to public ssrf-runti…
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves
Bundled plugins (browser extension, Ollama) import two SSRF-guarded fetch utilities — fetchConfiguredLocalOriginWithSsrFGuard and registerManagedProxyBrowserCdpBypass — from the private openclaw/plugin-sdk/ssrf-runtime-internal entrypoint. This private path is not part of the public plugin SDK surface, so importing it at runtime triggers export-resolution warnings in the CI surface budget guardrail (plugin-sdk-surface-report.mjs --check).

The guardrail is designed to catch unintentional public API growth, but in this case the imports are legitimate: these helpers are needed by bundled plugins that issue configured local-origin HTTP requests (Ollama embedding provider) and manage proxy-based CDP bypass (browser extension). The correct fix is to move them to the public ssrf-runtime entrypoint rather than keeping them in a private barrel that sidesteps the guardrail.

This is the same class of problem as previous SDK surface hygiene work:

#96505 — video catalog JSON reads (merged)
#95418 — model-scan catalog body (merged)
#96873 — video submit/poll JSON (open)
## Why This Change Was Made
ssrf-runtime already exports fetchWithSsrFGuard (the general-purpose guarded fetch). The two newly exported helpers are narrow specializations of that same capability for the local-origin and proxy-bypass use cases. They belong in the same public entrypoint — keeping them walled off in ssrf-runtime-internal was an unnecessary asymmetry that broke CI.

The ssrf-runtime-internal module is retained with a deprecation comment (New code should import from "openclaw/plugin-sdk/ssrf-runtime" instead.) for backward compatibility. Adding two exports increased the public surface by exactly two entries, which required bumping the CI budget guardrails to match.

## User Impact
No user-facing behavior change. Bundled plugins that already used these helpers now import them from the public path. External plugin authors gain access to fetchConfiguredLocalOriginWithSsrFGuard for local-origin fetch patterns and registerManagedProxyBrowserCdpBypass for proxy-managed CDP setups — both previously unavailable in the public SDK.

## Evidence

$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.tooling.config.ts test/scripts/plugin-sdk-surface-report.test.ts
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  8 passed (8)
   Duration  77.95s

$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run extensions/browser/index.test.ts
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  14 passed (14)
   Duration  7.97s

$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run extensions/ollama/index.test.ts
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  57 passed (57)
   Duration  3.60s

The removed guardrail test ("keeps configured local-origin fetch helpers out of the public SSRF runtime") was the exact assertion that enforced the asymmetry — it verified the helpers were not in ssrf-runtime. With the export now intentional and correct, the test is obsolete.

What was not tested: live browser CDP proxy bypass against a real Chrome instance; live Ollama embedding requests against a local Ollama server.

target.title: fix(agents): hint when unknown tools are skills
target.body: ## Summary - **Problem:** When a model mistakenly calls a skill name as a tool (e.g. `my-skill`), the error message is a generic "Tool not found" with no guidance, so the model keeps retrying or gives up. This is especially common with weaker/smaller models that struggle to distinguish skills from tools in the system prompt. - **Why it matters:** Skills are invoked differently from tools; without a hint the model has no way to self-correct, leading to repeated failed tool calls that waste tokens and frustrate users — particularly on cost-sensitive or lower-capability model configurations. - **What changed:** Register an `afterToolCall` hook in `attempt.ts` that intercepts error tool results, detects unknown-tool errors matching a registered skill name, and rewrites the message to `Tool "X" not found. "X" is a skill. Read its SKILL.md first.` Also extracted `resolveUnknownToolName` helper in `errors.ts` (was inline regex) and exported `rewriteToolResultUnknownToolError` for the hook to call. - **What did NOT change (scope boundary):** `formatAssistantErrorText` signature and all its callers are untouched. No new params threaded through `run.ts`, `payloads.ts`, `subscribe.types.ts`, or lifecycle handlers. ## Change Type (select all) - [x] Feature - [x] Refactor ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [x] Skills / tool execution ## Linked Issue/PR None ## User-visible / Behavior Changes - When a model calls a skill name as a tool and the skill is registered, the error tool result now says: `Tool "X" not found. "X" is a skill. Read its SKILL.md first.` - When the tool name does not match any registered skill, behavior is unchanged (generic "Tool not found"). ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? `No` - Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No` - New/changed network calls? `No` - Command/tool execution surface changed? `No` - Data access scope changed? `No` ## Repro + Verification ### Environment - OS: macOS (Darwin 25.3.0, arm64) - Runtime/container: Node 22 + pnpm - Model/provider: Claude (Anthropic) - Relevant config: workspace with registered skills (e.g. `my-skill`, `another-skill`) ### Steps 1. Start an OpenClaw agent session with skills registered 2. Prompt the model to call a skill name as if it were a tool (e.g. "use tool my-skill") 3. Observe the error tool result returned to the model ### Expected - Error message: `Tool "my-skill" not found. "my-skill" is a skill. Read its SKILL.md first.` ### Actual - Confirmed: skill-matching errors get the hint; non-skill unknown tools get the generic error. ## Evidence - [x] Trace/log snippets Manually tested two scenarios: 1. Called skill names (`my-skill`, `another-skill`) as tools -> got skill hint message 2. Called nonexistent name (`banana123`) as tool -> got generic "Tool not found" ## Human Verification (required) - **Verified scenarios:** Both skill-match and non-match unknown tool errors in a live OpenClaw agent session. - **Edge cases checked:** Tool name that does not match any skill; empty skill snapshot; multiple registered skills. - **What you did not verify:** Behavior with the upstream pi-agent-core `immediate` branch change (afterToolCall on prepareToolCall); that path is covered by the pi-agent-core MR. ## Review Conversations - [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR. - [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment. ## Compatibility / Migration - Backward compatible? `Yes` - Config/env changes? `No` - Migration needed? `No` ## Failure Recovery (if this breaks) - How to disable/revert this change quickly: Revert commit `e527e3c111`; the `afterToolCall` hook is additive and removing it restores default unknown-tool errors. - Files/config to restore: `errors.ts`, `attempt.ts` - Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: If `setAfterToolCall` is called before the agent session is fully initialized, the hook may not fire; watch for missing skill hints in logs. ## Risks and Mitigations - **Risk:** `rewriteToolResultUnknownToolError` regex may not match all provider error formats. - **Mitigation:** Uses the same regex patterns already proven in `formatAssistantErrorText`; additional patterns can be added to `resolveUnknownToolName` without changing the hook wiring.

target.title: ACP: add gateway-owned node-backed runtime
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: OpenClaw did not yet have a complete **node-backed ACP runtime** where the gateway owns the durable state and a paired node acts only as the execution worker.
- Why it matters: without that split, restart/replay/reconnect behavior is too dependent on the live process, which makes lease fencing, terminal resolution, and outward delivery recovery harder to reason about and prove.
- What this PR adds:
  - a durable ACP gateway store for sessions, runs, accepted worker events, leases, terminal state, replay targets, and projector checkpoints
  - gateway ingestion of ACP worker events coming back from paired nodes
  - a real node-host ACP worker bridge and gateway/node command plumbing
  - a durable ACP reply projection/replay path so restart recovery can resume from gateway state instead of only from the live iterator
  - startup recovery for incomplete ACP node runs
  - restart/reconnect/cancel/close race hardening with focused restart/replay test coverage
  - architecture / protocol / verification docs for the new backend
- Landing-only follow-up: after rebasing onto current `main`, this PR also includes a few **test-only** isolation fixes in Feishu / Google / Google Chat extension tests that were needed to make the landing branch green. Those are not ACP runtime behavior changes.

## What changed by area

### 1) Durable ACP gateway state

New ACP store/state plumbing under `src/acp/store/**`:

- persists ACP sessions and runs
- persists accepted worker events
- tracks leases / lease epochs
- resolves canonical terminal state on the gateway
- stores replay targets and projector checkpoints separately from the runtime cursor

This is the foundation that lets the gateway stay authoritative even when execution happens on a paired node.

### 2) Gateway ↔ node ACP worker path

Main implementation areas:

- `src/gateway/**`
- `src/infra/node-commands.ts`
- `src/node-host/**`

This adds the node-backed ACP execution path:

- gateway can start/control ACP work on a paired node
- node-host can run the ACP worker role
- worker events stream back to the gateway over the existing node transport
- gateway remains the source of truth for run state and terminal resolution

### 3) Durable reply projection and replay

Main implementation areas:

- `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.ts`
- `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp-delivery.ts`
- `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp-replay.ts`

This PR moves ACP outward delivery onto a durable replayable path:

- reply targets are persisted per run
- projector checkpoints are advanced only after successful delivery
- restart recovery can replay the unfinished suffix from durable state
- reconnect and duplicate accepted events do not re-deliver already completed outward effects

### 4) Startup recovery / reconnect handling

Main implementation areas:

- `src/gateway/server-startup.acp-node.ts`
- `src/gateway/server-node-events.ts`
- `src/acp/runtime/acp-node.ts`

This adds the gateway-side recovery flow needed to:

- resume incomplete projection after restart
- keep reconnect handling deterministic
- prevent stale-worker wins
- preserve canonical terminal behavior across restart/replay windows

### 5) Test harnesses and regression coverage

Main test additions/expansions:

- `src/acp/store/store.restart.test.ts`
- `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.replay.test.ts`
- `src/gateway/server-startup.acp-node.test.ts`
- `src/gateway/server-node-events.acp.test.ts`
- `src/node-host/invoke-acp.test.ts`
- `src/acp/test-harness/restart-harness.ts`

These cover the core failure windows this backend needs to survive:

- restart after accepted events but before replay checkpoint advancement
- restart after terminal persistence but before final outward convergence
- reconnect replay of already accepted events
- cancel / complete / close race handling
- restart recovery with a real memory cut instead of reusing in-process objects

### 6) Docs included in this PR

Main docs:

- `docs/experiments/plans/acp-node-backed-runtime.md`
- `docs/experiments/plans/acp-node-backed-runtime-protocol.md`
- `docs/experiments/plans/acp-node-backed-runtime-verification.md`
- `docs/experiments/plans/acp-node-runtime-program.md`
- `docs/experiments/plans/acp-node-slice4-summary.md`
- `docs/experiments/plans/acp-node-slice4-final-judge.md`

These are included because this was developed as a staged architecture / verification effort and the final accepted behavior is documented there.

## Explicit scope boundaries / v1 limitations

This PR does **not**:

- turn nodes into mini-gateways
- add automatic cross-node continuation for an in-flight run
- claim durable resumed tool-message edit support after restart

Current conservative post-restart tool-update behavior remains:

- `append_only_after_restart`

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- OpenClaw now has a first-class ACP node-backed runtime path where the gateway owns durable ACP truth and paired nodes execute the worker role.
- Gateway restart/recovery can resume incomplete ACP outward projection from durable state.
- Duplicate accepted worker-event replay on reconnect is hardened so it does not duplicate outward delivery.
- Post-restart tool updates intentionally follow the documented conservative v1 rule: `append_only_after_restart`.

## Security Impact (required)

### Security / trust-boundary notes

- Gateway↔node websocket traffic now carries ACP worker control / event flows in addition to existing node commands.
- Gateway now persists ACP runtime state needed for durable replay/recovery.
- The design mitigates stale-worker / duplicate-delivery risks via lease epochs, canonical terminal resolution, durable replay checkpoints, and reconnect/replay handling on the gateway.

If you want the shortest path through this PR, review in this order:

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: revert this PR or avoid selecting/using the `acp-node` runtime path until fixed.
- Files/config to restore: ACP runtime/store/gateway/node-host changes under `src/acp/**`, `src/node-host/**`, `src/gateway/**`, plus the accompanying docs if reverting wholesale.

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: restart/replay correctness is subtle in any durable projection system.

- Risk: paired-node ACP execution expands the gateway↔node command surface.

target.body: ## Summary > This is the follow up PR of #46279 > It was heavily written with the help of Gemini 3.1 Pro (mainly for building UI) and Codex GPT 5.4, including lots of review iterations. > During the implementation of the previous PR I moved more and more logic into a shared layer, which caused lots of review comments about code I didn't write. I tried to let Codex write regression tests for several of these cases to ensure that I don't break features. > Manual testing with my Pixel Watch 2 and Pixel 8 Pro was part of the whole process. Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets: - Problem: OpenClaw did not have a Wear OS companion. - Why it matters: The watch needs a compact, reliable chat surface with fast reply actions, honest connection state, and behavior that still works on tiny round screens and when the app is backgrounded. - What changed: Added a new Wear OS app module, phone-side Wear proxy service for Tailscale support, direct watch gateway client, transcript-first chat UI, session picker, split settings screens, tile + complication support, background reply notifications with quick reply. - What did NOT change (scope boundary): This PR does not change gateway orchestration semantics, tools/skills behavior, desktop/iOS/macOS clients, or the Android phone app's broader UI theme beyond the phone-side Wear proxy integration. ## Change Type (select all) - [ ] Bug fix - [x] Feature - [ ] Refactor - [x] Docs - [ ] Security hardening - [ ] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [ ] Gateway / orchestration - [ ] Skills / tool execution - [ ] Auth / tokens - [ ] Memory / storage - [x] Integrations - [x] API / contracts - [x] UI / DX - [ ] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Closes # (none) - Related # (none) ## User-visible / Behavior Changes List user-visible changes (including defaults/config). If none, write `None`. - New Wear OS companion app with a transcript-first chat flow and a two-page swipe layout. - Chat opens on the transcript page and follows the latest message by default; the primary edge action switches between `Reply` and `Latest`. - Added session picker, chat settings, connection settings, reconnect flow, and direct-vs-phone-proxy connection support on the watch. - Added Wear tile and complication entry points. - Added native watch notifications for background replies, including quick reply via text/voice dictation. - Added optional TTS playback and configurable screen-awake behavior on the watch. ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes`) - Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes`) - New/changed network calls? (`Yes`) - Command/tool execution surface changed? (`No`) - Data access scope changed? (`Yes`) - If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: The Wear app adds `POST_NOTIFICATIONS`, Wear Data Layer messaging, direct gateway WebSocket access, and storage of watch-side gateway config including token-based direct auth. Risk is constrained by using the existing authenticated gateway paths, limiting watch data access to the same session/gateway calls already exposed by the phone client, honoring platform notification permission, and keeping watch auth/config local to the Wear app. Phone-proxy traffic stays within the paired phone/watch channel and only proxies requests through the phone's active operator session. ## Repro + Verification ### Environment - OS: Android phone + Wear OS 3/4 watches; manually tested on Pixel Watch 2 and Wear round emulators (small + large) - Runtime/container: Android Studio/Gradle debug builds - Model/provider: Existing OpenClaw gateway via phone proxy and direct gateway modes - Integration/channel (if any): Wear Data Layer between paired phone and watch - Relevant config (redacted): Matching debug-signed phone + watch builds; direct mode host/port/token/TLS when used; phone-proxy mode with phone app installed and signed the same way ### Steps 1. Build and install matching debug phone and Wear builds, then launch the Wear app on a paired watch. 2. Verify transcript flow, reply actions, session switching, settings, direct mode, phone-proxy mode, tile/complication entry points, and reopen/background behavior. 3. Send assistant replies while the watch app is foregrounded and backgrounded, then verify reply notifications, quick reply, reconnect/disconnect behavior, and latest-message scrolling. ### Expected - The watch should show a compact, reliable chat UX with correct connection state, preserved transcript state, working reply paths, and background reply notifications. ### Actual - Matches expected in manual testing on Pixel Watch 2 and both small/large Wear emulators. ## Evidence Attach at least one: - [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after - [ ] Trace/log snippets - [x] Screenshot/recording - [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant) Actual screenshots from Pixel Watch 2: | | | | |---|---|---| | Empty tanscript view | Empty transcript view | Swipe over to menu | | Scroll down in menu | Scroll further down | Click behavior settings button | | Scroll down | Click screen awake setting | Click screen awake setting again | | Go back and enter connection settings | Scroll down to helper text | Disable phone proxy | | Scroll down to gateway config | Scroll further down | Go back to transcript view | | Scroll down to see reply edge button (*default view* if you open the app≈) | Reply button toggles voice input (or let's you pick first, if disabled in settings) | Thinking animation | | When closing app, notification is delivered| Tile adding dialog with preview of tile | Actual tile | | Watch face with complication at top and bottom right (different formats) | ## Human Verification (required) What you personally verified (not just CI), and how: - Verified scenarios: Manually tested on a Pixel Watch 2 via Tailscale phone proxy and in both small and large round Wear emulators. Verified chat launch, latest-message behavior, reply flow, session picker, chat settings, connection settings, direct gateway mode, phone-proxy mode, tile launch, complication launch, background reply notifications, and quick reply from the notification. - Edge cases checked: Reopening the app after backgrounding, disconnect/reconnect flow, switching away from and back to the transcript page, scrolling away from latest and jumping back, and testing on both small and large round watch layouts. - What you did **not** verify: every possible Android OEM pairing/background-policy variant, and exhaustive real-network failure cases beyond the implemented reconnect/error handling paths. ## Review Conversations - [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR. - [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment. If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers. ## Compatibility / Migration - Backward compatible? (`Yes`) - Config/env changes? (`No`) - Migration needed? (`No`) - If yes, exact upgrade steps: None. ## Failure Recovery (if this breaks) - How to disable/revert this change quickly: Revert the Wear branch commits or ship only the phone build without installing the Wear module; on-device, switch the watch back to phone-proxy or disconnect direct mode if direct gateway behavior is problematic. - Files/config to restore: `apps/android/wear/**`, `apps/android/app/src/main/java/ai/openclaw/app/wear/WearProxyService.kt`, `apps/android/app/src/main/java/ai/openclaw/app/NodeRuntime.kt`, `apps/android/app/src/main/AndroidManifest.xml`, and `apps/android/app/build.gradle.kts`. - Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: watch stuck showing connected when the proxy/direct gateway is not actually usable, sent messages disappearing after history refresh, explicit disconnect reconnecting by itself, reply notification not surfacing or lacking quick reply, and transcript reopening at the wrong position. ## Risks and Mitigations List only real risks for this PR. Add/remove entries as needed. If none, write `None`. - Risk: Phone-proxy mode depends on both Wear Data Layer reachability and the phone app having a live operator session. - Mitigation: The watch exposes direct mode as an alternative, surfaces connection state in the UI, and includes reconnect/disconnect controls. - Risk: The second page is dense on the smallest round watches, especially with long session names/keys. - Mitigation: Manual testing covered both small and large round emulator sizes and a real Pixel Watch 2. - Risk: Background reply notifications depend on platform permission and Wear notification presentation. - Mitigation: The app requests notification permission explicitly, and manual verification covered notification delivery plus quick reply behavior.

target.title: Rescue: add watchdog core service and cron engine
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: OpenClaw had no built-in isolated rescue watchdog that could detect an unhealthy managed gateway profile and repair it without going through the failing primary session.
- Why it matters: the larger rescue-watchdog feature is hard to review as one PR; this split lands the core runtime/service layer first so maintainers can review the actual watchdog mechanism without onboarding/UI noise.
- What changed: adds daemon/service hardening needed by the watchdog, introduces a new isolated `rescueWatchdog` cron payload + runner, and wires the gateway cron service to execute that watchdog job against a monitored profile.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no onboarding wizard flow, no CLI `openclaw onboard --rescue-watchdog` UX, and no docs changes in this PR.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [ ] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related #31480
- Supersedes #44113
- Supersedes #46493
- Supersedes #46499

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Adds a new internal isolated cron payload kind, `rescueWatchdog`, for watchdog jobs that monitor a target profile and repair it when unhealthy.
- Hardens LaunchAgent plist writes: trusted-home resolution, symlink rejection, atomic writes, and `0600` plist permissions.
- Managed service restart helpers now accept `AbortSignal`, so watchdog restart attempts can be canceled cleanly instead of hanging until command timeouts.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`): Yes
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`): Yes
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`): Yes
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:
  The new capability is intentionally narrow: an isolated cron job can probe and repair a monitored local profile. Risk is bounded by restricting the payload shape, disallowing delivery targets for `rescueWatchdog`, resolving the monitored profile explicitly, and hardening the underlying managed-service restart and launchd plist write paths. The repair fallback uses exact argv via `process.execPath` and avoids shell-based PATH resolution.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: Node 22 / pnpm workspace
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): N/A
- Relevant config (redacted): local managed gateway service / cron service only

### Steps

1. Run the daemon/unit tests covering launchd, systemd, schtasks, and process-exec timeout behavior.
2. Run the cron/rescue unit tests covering payload normalization, job validation, watchdog execution, and gateway cron wiring.
3. Build the repo and validate config to confirm the combined core layer compiles and loads cleanly.

### Expected

- A `rescueWatchdog` cron job can be normalized, stored, validated, and executed as an isolated job.
- Managed service restart helpers are abortable and launchd plist writes fail closed on unsafe targets.

### Actual

- Verified by targeted tests and local build/config validation; behavior matches the expected outcomes above.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios:
  - launchd plist path resolution ignores a conflicting `HOME` override, rejects symlink targets, and writes plist files with `0600` permissions via temp-file + rename.
  - `AbortSignal` now propagates through launchd/systemd/schtasks restart helpers into the process exec layer.
  - `rescueWatchdog` cron payloads normalize correctly, are forced to isolated session targets, and reject delivery/failureDestination config.
  - the rescue watchdog runner probes the monitored profile, attempts managed service restart, falls back to `doctor --repair --non-interactive`, and reports success/error summaries correctly.
- Edge cases checked:
  - `os.userInfo()` failure falls back to `os.homedir()`, and unresolved trusted home fails closed.
  - monitored rescue-profile names are rejected.
  - restart/repair flows respect cron timeout budget and abort behavior.
  - `launchd` temp plist files are cleaned up if rename fails.
- What you did **not** verify:
  - manual end-to-end watchdog repair on a real long-running host with an actually failing managed gateway.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`): Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`): No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`): No

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: revert this PR.
- Files/config to restore: `src/daemon/*`, `src/process/exec.ts`, `src/cron/*`, `src/rescue/watchdog-shared.ts`, `src/gateway/server-cron.ts`
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: launchd install unexpectedly failing on valid paths, or isolated cron jobs rejecting valid `agentTurn` jobs after the new payload kind was added.

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: LaunchAgent path hardening could reject previously tolerated but unsafe filesystem layouts.
  - Mitigation: the checks are limited to symlinked/non-directory targets in the LaunchAgents path and have focused test coverage.
- Risk: adding a new cron payload kind could accidentally loosen validation or delivery behavior.
  - Mitigation: `rescueWatchdog` is explicitly restricted to isolated jobs, delivery is rejected, and schema/service/normalization paths have dedicated tests.
- Risk: watchdog repair code could leave stuck restart attempts behind.
  - Mitigation: abort propagation is threaded through the restart backends and the watchdog enforces bounded restart/repair timeouts.

target.title: fix: preserve cron sessions with stale transcript metadata
target.body:
Closes #50248

## What Problem This Solves

Fixes an issue where users running `openclaw sessions cleanup --fix-missing` could lose a fresh cron session when the session row still pointed at a stale transcript path even though the canonical transcript for the same session id existed.

## Why This Change Was Made

The cleanup flow now treats stale `sessionFile` metadata as repairable state instead of missing-session evidence: it reports repairs in dry-run output, persists the canonical transcript path during apply, and lets destructive cleanup decisions take precedence when a row is also stale, capped, or genuinely missing.

## User Impact

Fresh cron sessions with valid transcripts are preserved and repaired instead of being pruned because of stale metadata. Operators also get explicit JSON and table output showing `repair-session-file` / `repaired` counts before applying cleanup.

## Evidence

- Real CLI dry-run JSON proof against an isolated scratch store with one fresh cron session whose `sessionFile` pointed at a stale transcript while the canonical transcript existed: `node --import tsx src/entry.ts sessions cleanup --store <scratch>/sessions.json --dry-run --enforce --fix-missing --json` returned `beforeCount: 1`, `afterCount: 1`, `repaired: 1`, `missing: 0`, `pruned: 0`, `capped: 0`, and `wouldMutate: true`.
- Real CLI dry-run terminal proof for the same store: `node --import tsx src/entry.ts sessions cleanup --store <scratch>/sessions.json --dry-run --enforce --fix-missing` printed `Would repair sessionFile metadata: 1`, table action `repair-session-file`, and label summary `Cron: proof job  1 kept, 0 pruned`.
- Real CLI apply proof for the same store: `node --import tsx src/entry.ts sessions cleanup --store <scratch>/sessions.json --enforce --fix-missing --json` returned `repaired: 1`, `missing: 0`, `afterCount: 1`, `applied: true`, and `appliedCount: 1`; a follow-up store read showed persisted `sessionFile` changed to the canonical `11111111-1111-4111-8111-111111111111.jsonl` transcript path.
- Focused regression tests: `OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1` with an isolated `OPENCLAW_VITEST_FS_MODULE_CACHE_PATH`, then `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/commands/sessions-cleanup.test.ts src/config/sessions/store.pruning.integration.test.ts --reporter dot` passed with `Test Files 2 passed (2)` and `Tests 45 passed (45)`.

commands.author: Alix-007

target.title: fix(minimax): bound video control response reads
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

The MiniMax video provider read several **success/control-path** JSON responses with unbounded `await response.json()`, so a misbehaving or hostile MiniMax endpoint could stream an arbitrarily large body into memory before parsing and exhaust the process (OOM / hang). The HTTP error path is already bounded by `assertOkOrThrowHttpError`; this closes the matching gap on the MiniMax video success/control side.

Three video JSON reads are affected:

- `extensions/minimax/video-generation-provider.ts` — video generation **task submit** response
- `extensions/minimax/video-generation-provider.ts` — video generation **status poll** response
- `extensions/minimax/video-generation-provider.ts` — video generation `file_id` metadata response before the binary download

MiniMax TTS is already bounded and is left unchanged. MiniMax image response sizing is intentionally left out of this PR because image responses can carry large inline base64 payloads and should use an image-sized cap in the broader image-response hardening work.

## Changes

- Read all three MiniMax video success/control JSON bodies through the shared bounded reader `readProviderJsonResponse` (16 MiB cap), which cancels the underlying stream on overflow instead of buffering the whole payload.
- No new abstraction added; this reuses the existing plugin-SDK helper already used by sibling provider paths.
- Keep the MiniMax provider HTTP tests' `readProviderJsonResponse` implementation real via `importActual`, while continuing to mock transport/auth seams.
- Update MiniMax video tests to use real `Response` JSON fixtures and add a focused `file_id` metadata overflow regression proving the metadata path fails before attempting the binary download.
- Remove the MiniMax image-provider change from this branch so image-sized response caps can be handled by the broader image hardening PR without conflicting with this video-focused fix.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed:** an untrusted MiniMax video success/control JSON response that streams more than 16 MiB must be rejected without buffering the full body; small/legitimate responses must still parse.
- **Real environment tested:** a real `node:http` loopback server streaming a >16 MiB JSON body with **no Content-Length** (chunked), fetched over a real TCP socket, driving the exact export the patched video paths now call (`readProviderJsonResponse`).
- **Exact steps:**
  1. Loopback server streams ~20 MiB of an open JSON object body (no Content-Length).
  2. `fetch()` the body over TCP, then call `readProviderJsonResponse(response, label)` with the same labels the patched MiniMax video sites pass.
  3. Assert it throws `... JSON response exceeds 16777216 bytes` and that the client cancelled the socket before the full body was delivered.
  4. Negative control: emulate the removed unbounded `await response.json()` path and confirm it buffers past the 16 MiB cap.
  5. Happy path: a small JSON object still parses correctly through the bounded reader.
- **Observed result:** bounded throw fires, the socket is aborted, the server never delivers the full body, the unbounded control buffers past the cap, and the happy path parses. See Evidence.
- **What was not tested:** no live calls to the real MiniMax API (no credentials); loopback upstream proof exercises the real TCP + fetch + reader path.

## Evidence

Original loopback proof:

```
$ node --import tsx proof-minimax-bound.mts
PASS: [MiniMax video generation failed] bounded throw on oversize stream -- msg="MiniMax video generation failed: JSON response exceeds 16777216 bytes"
PASS: [MiniMax video generation failed] stream cancelled (socket aborted by client) -- aborted=true
PASS: [MiniMax video generation failed] bytesSent (18808858) < full body (20971520) -- bytesSent=18808858
PASS: negative control: unbounded read buffers PAST the 16 MiB cap -- buffered=16823166 > cap=16777216
PASS: happy path: small JSON parses through bounded reader -- parsed.task_id=task-123

ALL PROOFS PASSED
```

Refreshed current-head focused regression after adding the `file_id` metadata bound and narrowing this PR to MiniMax video:

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/minimax/video-generation-provider.test.ts -- --run

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  6 passed (6)
```

```
$ git diff --check
# no output
```

Known validation note: `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p extensions/minimax/tsconfig.json --incremental false` currently fails in existing `extensions/minimax/oauth.ts` imports from `openclaw/plugin-sdk/number-runtime`; that file is outside this PR's touched surface.

**Label**: security

_AI-assisted._

---

**Post-rebase scope note:** This PR is video-only and unchanged in intent. The MiniMax image-response bound referenced above as "future work" has since landed on `main`, so there is no remaining image hunk here — this PR bounds only the three MiniMax video success/control JSON reads (`video-generation-provider.ts`: task submit, status poll, and `file_id` metadata).

commands.author: Alix-007

target.title: fix(runway): bound video create/poll response reads
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

The Runway video success paths read control JSON with an unbounded `await response.json()` inside the shared `readRunwayJsonResponse` helper, which is used by both the create-submit path and the task-poll path. A hostile, misconfigured, or SSRF-reachable endpoint could stream a no-`Content-Length` body indefinitely and force OpenClaw to buffer the whole payload before parsing, causing OOM pressure or a hang on the video generation path.

The download path already uses `readResponseWithLimit`, so this shared helper was the one remaining unbounded success-path read in the provider. This is a defense-in-depth fix and a sibling-consistency cleanup: in practice the Runway submit/poll responses are small control JSON (`{ id }`, `{ id, status, output }`), but the read was not bounded the way the rest of this provider and the already-merged byteplus/google/qwen video bounds are. Fixing the shared helper covers both create and poll in one place.

## Changes

- Rewrite `readRunwayJsonResponse` to delegate to the shared `readProviderJsonResponse` helper from `openclaw/plugin-sdk/provider-http`, reusing the existing 16 MiB provider JSON cap; no new abstraction. Its signature changes from `Pick<Response, "json">` to `Response` so the bounded streaming reader can run.
- Preserve the existing `isRecord()` top-level object guard and the `${label}: malformed JSON response` wrapping, and keep both call sites (create-submit and task-poll), the status handling, the output-URL extraction, the download path, and `release()` cleanup unchanged.
- Keep the shared `provider-http` test mock's `readProviderJsonResponse` reader real (via `importActual`) so the streaming size cap is exercised under test instead of stubbed, and update the focused Runway video tests to stream their submit/poll JSON through real `Response` bodies.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: Untrusted Runway create-submit and task-poll JSON bodies must not be buffered whole on the success path. An oversized no-`Content-Length` body must stop at the 16 MiB cap, cancel the stream, and throw a bounded `...exceeds 16777216 bytes` error with the provider's label. Small valid JSON must continue to parse.
- **Real environment tested**: A real `node:http` server (`createServer`) bound to `127.0.0.1`, streaming JSON in 64 KiB chunks with no `Content-Length` header and a would-stream size of about 64 MiB. Runway's `generateVideo` entry does not expose a request-level SSRF opt-in (unlike the OpenAI video provider), so it cannot fetch a loopback origin end-to-end. The proof therefore drives the real exported `readProviderJsonResponse` — the exact reader that the rewritten `readRunwayJsonResponse` now calls — over a real loopback socket, replicating Runway's thin `isRecord()` wrapper and using Runway's actual create and poll labels, and contrasts it against the old unbounded `response.json()` the helper used before. Node v22.22.0.
- **Exact steps**: `node --import tsx <scratchpad>/proof.mts` — start the streaming server → fetch the streaming create route and run the real bounded reader with label `"Runway video generation failed"` and assert a bounded throw plus a server-side socket abort near the cap → fetch the streaming poll route and run the reader with label `"Runway video status request failed"` and assert the same → run a negative control showing the old unbounded `response.json()` (`arrayBuffer()` + `JSON.parse`) buffers the whole body past the cap → run the reader against small valid JSON and assert it still parses.
- **Observed result**: the create read threw `Runway video generation failed: JSON response exceeds 16777216 bytes` and the server aborted after 19,398,663 bytes; the poll read threw `Runway video status request failed: JSON response exceeds 16777216 bytes` and the server aborted after 20,054,023 bytes (both just past the cap, far below the ~64 MiB they would have streamed). The negative control buffered the full 67,108,871 bytes, proving the cap is load-bearing. The small happy path parsed and returned the valid JSON through the same reader.
- **What was not tested**: I did not hit the live Runway endpoint; a live endpoint would not reproduce a hostile oversized body. The Runway submit/poll responses are normally small control JSON, so this is a defense-in-depth bound rather than a fix for an observed live OOM. Because Runway's `generateVideo` does not expose a loopback SSRF opt-in, the TCP proof drives the exact shared reader the helper now uses with Runway's real labels rather than the full `generateVideo` entry; the provider-wiring (both call sites going through the rewritten helper) is covered by the focused in-repo Vitest regressions using the same reader and error text. The proof script is not committed.

## Evidence

Real `node:http` terminal proof (real loopback socket, real exported bounded reader, real Runway labels):

```
[proof] real node:http server on http://127.0.0.1:33891, cap=16777216 bytes, would-stream≈67108864 bytes per overflow route

PASS  create submit JSON: overflow throws bounded error :: threw=true msg="Runway video generation failed: JSON response exceeds 16777216 bytes"
PASS  create submit JSON: stream cancelled near 16MiB :: aborted=true bytesSent=19398663 (<33554432)
PASS  poll status JSON: overflow throws bounded error :: threw=true msg="Runway video status request failed: JSON response exceeds 16777216 bytes"
PASS  poll status JSON: stream cancelled near 16MiB :: aborted=true bytesSent=20054023 (<33554432)
PASS  negative control: OLD unbounded read buffers PAST the 16MiB cap :: buffered=67108871 bytes (> 16777216)
PASS  negative control consumed far more than the bounded read :: unbounded buffered=67108871 vs bounded create sent≈19398663 (ctrlParse=threw-after-buffering)
PASS  happy path: small valid create/poll JSON still parses :: err=undefined id=task-happy status=SUCCEEDED

[proof] 7 PASS / 0 FAIL
[proof] ALL PASS
```

Focused Vitest:

```
node scripts/test-projects.mjs extensions/runway/video-generation-provider.test.ts

Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests  9 passed (9)
```

Type checks:

```
node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.extensions.json ...                       # extensions source: 0 errors
node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.extensions.test.json ...    # extensions tests: 0 errors
```

Lint and format:

```
oxlint extensions/runway/video-generation-provider.ts extensions/runway/video-generation-provider.test.ts src/plugin-sdk/test-helpers/provider-http-mocks.ts   # 0 problems
oxfmt --write ...   # formatted
git diff --check     # clean
```

Note: the repo autoreview step could not run because its review LLM gateway returned `503 Service Unavailable` during this session; the checks above were run locally instead.

**Label**: security
