target.title: fix(openai): bound video create-submit response reads
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

The OpenAI video create-submit success path read the control JSON with an unbounded `await response.json()`, so a hostile, misconfigured, or SSRF-reachable endpoint could stream a no-`Content-Length` body indefinitely and force OpenClaw to buffer the whole payload before parsing, causing OOM pressure or a hang on the video generation path.

The poll path already goes through the bounded `pollProviderOperationJson` helper and the download path already uses `readResponseWithLimit`, so the create-submit JSON read at `extensions/openai/video-generation-provider.ts` was the one remaining unbounded success-path read. This is a defense-in-depth fix and a sibling-consistency cleanup: in practice the OpenAI submit response is a small control JSON (`{ id, model, status, ... }`), but the read was not bounded the way the rest of this provider and the already-merged byteplus/google/qwen video bounds are.

## Changes

- Replace the create-submit `(await response.json()) as OpenAIVideoResponse` with the shared `readProviderJsonResponse` helper from `openclaw/plugin-sdk/provider-http`, reusing the existing 16 MiB provider JSON cap; no new abstraction.
- Preserve the existing `assertOkOrThrowHttpError` behavior, the `submitted.id` / status extraction, the poll and download paths, and the `release()` cleanup.
- Keep the shared `provider-http` test mock's `readProviderJsonResponse` reader real (via `importActual`) so the streaming size cap is exercised under test instead of stubbed, and update the focused OpenAI video tests to stream their submit/poll JSON through real `Response` bodies.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: An untrusted OpenAI video create-submit JSON body must not be buffered whole on the success path. An oversized no-`Content-Length` body must stop at the 16 MiB cap, cancel the stream, and throw a bounded `...exceeds 16777216 bytes` error. Small valid JSON must continue to parse and the full poll + download path must keep working.
- **Real environment tested**: A real `node:http` server (`createServer`) bound to `127.0.0.1`, streaming JSON in 64 KiB chunks with no `Content-Length` header and a would-stream size of about 64 MiB. The script drove the real exported `buildOpenAIVideoGenerationProvider().generateVideo()` over a real loopback socket through the real `postJsonRequest`, guarded fetch path, and `readProviderJsonResponse`, using the provider's request-level `allowPrivateNetwork` opt-in for the loopback target. Node v22.22.0.
- **Exact steps**: `node --import tsx <scratchpad>/proof.mts` — start the streaming server → call the real provider create-submit against the streaming route and assert a bounded throw plus a server-side socket abort near the cap → run a negative control using the old unbounded `fetch().arrayBuffer()` against the same body → call the real provider against a small valid submit JSON and drive the full poll + download path.
- **Observed result**: the create-submit read threw `OpenAI video generation failed: JSON response exceeds 16777216 bytes` and the server aborted after 17,825,799 bytes (just past the cap, far below the ~64 MiB it would have streamed). The negative control buffered the full 67,108,871 bytes, proving the cap is load-bearing. The small happy path parsed the submit JSON and returned the generated video through the real poll + download path.
- **What was not tested**: I did not hit the live OpenAI Sora endpoint; a live endpoint would not reproduce a hostile oversized body. This OpenAI submit response is normally a small control JSON, so this is a defense-in-depth bound rather than a fix for an observed live OOM. The real socket proof covers the transport, the cap, and the cancellation behavior; the provider-wiring is also covered by the focused in-repo Vitest regressions using the same shared reader and error text. The proof script is not committed.

## Evidence

[proof] 5 PASS / 0 FAIL

target.title: fix(xai): bound video response reads
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

On the success path, the xAI video generation provider read the create and poll JSON responses with an unbounded `await response.json()`. The response body comes from an external, untrusted endpoint, so a hostile or buggy server that streams a very large body with no `Content-Length` could force the runtime to buffer the entire payload into memory before parsing it — an OOM / hang vector. This mirrors the sibling provider hardening landed in #96604–#96608 and #96875.

## Changes

- `extensions/xai/video-generation-provider.ts`: `readXaiVideoJson` (used by both the create and poll paths) now reads the body through the shared bounded reader `readResponseWithLimit` (16 MiB cap) and parses the resulting bytes, instead of `await response.json()`. On overflow it throws `xAI video generation response exceeds 16777216 bytes` and cancels the stream; malformed/non-object payloads keep the existing `xAI video generation response malformed` behavior. The binary video download path already used `readResponseWithLimit` and is unchanged.
- `extensions/xai/video-generation-provider.test.ts`: existing mocks switched to real streamed `Response` bodies, plus two new regressions that drive an unbounded create/poll body and assert the bounded throw and stream cancellation.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: The xAI video create and poll control JSON from an untrusted external endpoint must not be buffered whole on the success path. An oversized no-`Content-Length` body must stop at the 16 MiB cap, cancel the stream, and throw a bounded `...exceeds 16777216 bytes` error. Small valid JSON must continue to parse normally.
- **Real environment tested**: A real `node:http` server (`createServer`) bound to `127.0.0.1`, streaming JSON in 64 KiB chunks with no `Content-Length` and a would-stream size of ≈64 MiB. The script drove the provider's exact success-path wrapper `readXaiVideoJson` — reproduced byte-for-byte, calling the same exported bounded reader `readResponseWithLimit` from `openclaw/plugin-sdk/response-limit-runtime` with the identical 16 MiB cap and overflow error text — over a real loopback fetch + socket, for both the create and poll routes. Node v22.22.0.
- **Exact steps**: `node --import tsx scratchpad/xai-bound-proof.mts` — start the streaming server → call the create-path JSON reader and assert bounded throw + server-side socket abort near the cap → call the poll-path JSON reader and assert the same → run a negative control using old unbounded `fetch().arrayBuffer()` against the same streaming body → call the reader against a small valid JSON response.
- **Observed result after fix**: create JSON threw `xAI video generation response exceeds 16777216 bytes` and aborted after 19,464,207 bytes; poll JSON threw the same and aborted after 19,136,527 bytes. The negative control buffered the full 67,108,879 bytes, proving the cap is load-bearing. The small happy path parsed `request_id=req_happy status=done`. Focused Vitest: 15 passed.
- **What was not tested**: I did not hit the live xAI service; a live endpoint would not reproduce a hostile oversized body. Because the xAI video provider hard-codes `allowPrivateNetwork: false` and does not expose a per-request SSRF policy, the full exported provider cannot be driven over a loopback socket (the SSRF guard rejects 127.0.0.1); the TCP proof therefore drives the provider's exact success-path JSON wrapper and the real shared bounded reader over a real socket, and the create/poll provider wiring is covered by the focused in-repo Vitest regressions using the same reader and error text. In practice the control JSON is small; this is defense-in-depth. The proof script is not committed.

## Evidence

Real `node:http` terminal proof (real loopback socket, exact provider success-path wrapper, real shared bounded reader):

```

PASS  create JSON: stream cancelled near 16MiB :: aborted=true bytesSent=19464207 (<33554432)
PASS  poll JSON: overflow throws bounded error :: threw=true msg="xAI video generation response exceeds 16777216 bytes"
PASS  poll JSON: stream cancelled near 16MiB :: aborted=true bytesSent=19136527 (<33554432)
PASS  negative control: OLD unbounded read buffers PAST the 16MiB cap :: buffered=67108879 bytes (> 16777216)

PASS  happy path: small create JSON still parses :: request_id=req_happy status=done

[proof] 7 PASS / 0 FAIL

target.title: fix(oauth): bound github-copilot OAuth response reads at 16 MiB
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

`src/llm/utils/oauth/github-copilot.ts:178-190` (`fetchJson`) reads the OAuth response body with `await response.text()` and `response.json()`, both of which buffer the entire payload into memory before the runtime can decide to stop. A hostile or buggy GitHub OAuth endpoint (or enterprise proxy) can return `200 OK` with a body that grows unbounded, and the runtime will buffer all of it before failing. The existing `fetchResponse` timeout only caps wall-clock time, not bytes — a slow trickle that exceeds memory still OOMs.

The OAuth surface here is **untrusted**: `<domain>/login/device/code`, `<domain>/login/oauth/access_token`, `api.<domain>/copilot_internal/v2/token`, and `api.individual.githubcopilot.com/models` are all external, so a DoS-shaped response is in-scope as a runtime hardening fix.

## Changes

- `src/llm/utils/oauth/github-copilot.ts:7-9` — import `readProviderJsonResponse` and `readProviderTextResponse` from the shared `../../../agents/provider-http-errors.js` (sibling pattern matches `src/agents/chutes-oauth.ts:9`).
- `src/llm/utils/oauth/github-copilot.ts:182-197` — `fetchJson` swaps `await response.text()` / `response.json()` for the bounded readers with a per-call label `"GitHub Copilot <operation>"`. Default cap is the shared 16 MiB (`PROVIDER_TEXT_RESPONSE_MAX_BYTES` / `PROVIDER_JSON_RESPONSE_MAX_BYTES` from `src/agents/provider-http-errors.ts:14-17`).
- `src/llm/utils/oauth/github-copilot.test.ts:185-265` — 3 new inline tests in a new `describe("GitHub Copilot OAuth bounded reads")` block: (1) oversized streaming body triggers overflow with the labeled error and the standard 16 MiB cap, (2) normal-size OAuth JSON parses through, (3) happy-path round-trip through `refreshGitHubCopilotToken` returns the access token with `expires` as a number. Tests use real `ReadableStream` + chunked `vi.stubGlobal("fetch")`, mirroring the inline pattern from `src/agents/provider-transport-fetch.test.ts` (Alix-007 #96772).

No new helper, no SDK promotion, no new abstraction — pure per-surface application of an existing shared bounded reader.

## Why This Change Was Made

This is one of three SSE/stream bounded-read follow-ups I have queued (G1 = github-copilot OAuth here, E2 = mcp-http-fetch, future anthropic-oauth). They all reuse the same shared `readProvider{Text,Json}Response` helpers; the value is wiring each surface to them, not introducing a new bounded-read pattern per site.

The sibling reference already in `main` is `src/agents/chutes-oauth.ts:9` — same import shape, same helper, three call sites with labels `"Chutes userinfo"` / `"Chutes token exchange"` / `"Chutes token refresh"`. This PR mirrors that pattern with labels that reuse the existing `operation` parameter that each `fetchJson` call site already passes ("device code request", "device token request", "token refresh request", "model list request"), so the four call sites in this file stay distinct without further edits.

## Real behavior proof

Real Node `http` server on `127.0.0.1`, real `refreshGitHubCopilotToken` wrapper, `globalThis.fetch` rewritten to point `https://api.github.com/...` at the local server (production code untouched). Proof script is `/tmp/g1_real_server_proof.mts`, run once, not committed (per checklist #23).

- **Behavior addressed**: A 17 MiB OAuth response body triggers the 16 MiB cap on `readProviderJsonResponse` instead of buffering the full payload.
- **Real environment tested**: Linux Node v22.22.0, real `http.createServer` listening on ephemeral `127.0.0.1` port, real `globalThis.fetch` wrapper routing only the GitHub OAuth hostnames to localhost, real production `refreshGitHubCopilotToken` invoked.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  ```bash
  node --import tsx /tmp/g1_real_server_proof.mts
  ```
- **Evidence after fix** (3/3 PASS, captured from real terminal):
  ```
  PASS  negative-control: serverGotRequest=true bytesSent=17825792 cap=16777216 msg="GitHub Copilot token refresh request: JSON response exceeds 16777216 bytes"
  PASS  happy-path:        access=copilot-token-XYZ expires=1782658963000 expected=1782658963000 bytes=53 (cap=16777216)
  PASS  non-ok-status:     msg="401 Unauthorized: {\"error\":\"invalid_token\"}"

  Total: 3/3 passed
  ```
- **Observed result after fix**:
  - **Negative control**: server reported `bytesSent=17825792` (17 MiB) but the runtime threw `GitHub Copilot token refresh request: JSON response exceeds 16777216 bytes` instead of buffering all 17 MiB. The error message embeds the cap value and the per-call label so logs can attribute the bounded-read to this call site (not chutes, not anthropic).
  - **Happy path**: a 53-byte token-refresh response parses cleanly through the bounded reader and yields `access=copilot-token-XYZ` with `expires=1782658963000` (the 5-min bufferMs from `resolveExpiresAtMsFromEpochSeconds` is correctly applied).
  - **Non-OK status**: a 401 response with `{"error":"invalid_token"}` flows through `readProviderTextResponse` and is wrapped into the existing `${response.status} ${response.statusText}: ${text}` error format — same observable behavior as before, with the body now bounded at 16 MiB.
- **What was not tested**:
  - **Did not exercise a live GitHub OAuth endpoint** (a real server would never stream >16 MiB on these endpoints in the wild; the bounded-read contract is exactly about defending against adversarial/buggy servers, which the local Node server simulates faithfully).
  - **Did not test the device-code polling path** (lines 197 / 291 call sites) end-to-end — they share the same `fetchJson` helper so the wrapper-level guarantee is the same. Adding duplicate proof would inflate the PR without adding signal.
  - **Did not test Node 24** specifically (Node 22 used locally; `ReadableStream` and `TextEncoder` behavior is consistent across Node 22 LTS and Node 24).

## Out of scope

- `src/llm/utils/oauth/anthropic.ts:236` still has unbounded `await response.text()`. Different maintainer area; will be a follow-up PR with the same `readProviderTextResponse` swap.
- google.ts SDK-level fetch injection was considered for B1 but is not feasible: `@google/genai` 2.7.0 exposes `httpOptions: { baseUrl, apiVersion, headers, timeout, extraBody, retryOptions }` but no `fetch` parameter at the `GoogleGenAI` constructor, and `generateContentStream(params)` does not accept a second `RequestOptions` argument, so the SDK cannot be wired through `buildGuardedModelFetch`.
- E2 (mcp-http-fetch.ts:69) is a separate PR in the same campaign.

## Diff stats

```
2 files changed, 91 insertions(+), 2 deletions(-)
  src/llm/utils/oauth/github-copilot.ts         +9/-2   (1 import + 2 line changes in fetchJson + 2 WHY-comment lines)
  src/llm/utils/oauth/github-copilot.test.ts    +82/-0  (3 new inline tests, no new test file, no committed proof script)
```

Size: S (≤ 100 LoC non-test). All test additions are inline in the existing `github-copilot.test.ts`.

## Risk checklist

- **User-visible behavior change?** Yes — same shape, same errors. The only new failure mode is `GitHub Copilot <op>: JSON response exceeds 16777216 bytes` when an OAuth endpoint streams >16 MiB. That is the desired safety behavior.
- **Config/env/migration change?** No.
- **Security/auth/secrets/network change?** Yes, positively — adds 16 MiB body cap to a previously-unbounded OAuth response read. No new attack surface; no new endpoints touched.
- **Highest-risk area:** a misformed label could make the error unreadable. Mitigated by reusing the existing `operation` parameter (4 distinct, hand-formed strings already in production) and prefixing with `GitHub Copilot ` so logs disambiguate from sibling bounded reads (chutes, anthropic, etc.).

target.title: feat(media): force Telegram image generation as documents
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Fixes an issue where Telegram users receiving `image_generate` results could get generated images through photo-style delivery, which can recompress media and make generated image artifacts less reliable to save, inspect, or forward as original files.

## Why This Change Was Made

This change treats Telegram `image_generate` outputs as document/file media where supported. It keeps the existing direct Telegram delivery behavior and also propagates `forceDocument` through source-reply, block-reply, queued tool-media, and outbound delivery payload paths so the normal active generated-media reply path preserves the document-delivery preference too.

The change is intentionally scoped to delivery metadata. It does not alter image generation providers, media file creation, or non-Telegram channel behavior.

AI-assisted: yes; the final patch was reviewed with focused local tests and type/lint checks.

## User Impact

Telegram users should receive generated image outputs as document/file attachments instead of recompressed photo messages. That makes image-generation artifacts more faithful to the generated file and avoids losing file/document affordances in Telegram.

## Evidence

Local verification on commit `ffb71a9a42b3c9933215dc2141efd0157f03b2a9`:

- `git diff --check` passed.
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 <18 changed files>` passed.
- Focused tests passed: 357 tests across:
  - `src/agents/runtime-plan/types.compat.test.ts`
  - `src/infra/outbound/deliver.test.ts`
  - `src/infra/outbound/message-action-runner.send-validation.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/subagent-announce-delivery.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/embedded-agent-subscribe.handlers.messages.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/embedded-agent-subscribe.handlers.tools.media.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/embedded-agent-subscribe.handlers.tools.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.core.json --noEmit` passed.
- `pnpm build:plugin-sdk:dts` passed.
- `pnpm check:test-types` passed.
- Targeted oxlint on the changed TypeScript files passed with 0 warnings / 0 errors.

Operational note: full local `pnpm lint` was started but the small VM killed the extensions shard with SIGKILL after the core shard passed. `pnpm check:changed` attempted Blacksmith Testbox delegation, but this local environment does not have the `blacksmith` executable installed. CI should run those hosted gates on this updated head.

Native Telegram behavior proof is still required by repository policy; requesting a Mantis Telegram Desktop proof run in PR comments.

target.title: fix(cron): expose flat cron job fields and normalize malformed tool args
target.body:
Related: #3569 
Related: #64721

## What Problem This Solves

`src/agents/tools/cron-tool.ts` exposes a TypeBox schema to models in Openclaw where almost everything is `Type.Optional`. However the contract is enforced downstream in `normalize.ts` and `persisted-shape.ts` meaning fields that looked optional become required depending on which `kind` was chosen. The code says at ln 54–55:

> "Spell out job/patch properties for model-facing schema; runtime validation still happens in
> normalizeCronJob* to avoid nested union schemas."

In practice this means that openclaw gets no useful feedback from mistakes. Typical failures seen in testing: emitting the `job` argument as a truncated string instead of an object; supplying a top-level `mode` (valid only for the `wake` action); choosing a schedule kind without its required field (e.g. `kind: "cron"` with no `expr`, or omitting `payload.kind`); and passing `toolsAllow`/`fallbacks` as a scalar string instead of an array. Rejection gives generic error messages such as "jobId required" and "job required" with no field-level detail, so the openclaw doesn't have sufficient information to fix the problem. 

## Why This Change Was Made

I mostly use local Gemma 4 26b Q5 which became  completely unable to amend my cron jobs following upgrade to v6 Openclaw. However this is not simply a local LLM issue as Codex also had difficulties. Codex's greater reasoning power allowed for workrounds but with clearer schema these workarounds and iterative fixes wouldn't be needed.

I see that #3569 reported the same issues and the report was later closed through PR #10776. But #10776 did not address the TypeBox schema. @gerroon reported that fails still happen on their Qwen-30b-Instruct after the fix.

Also #64721 reports the same kind of problem: `payload.model`, `payload.timeoutSeconds`,
`payload.contextTokens`, and `maxSpendUsd` are accepted at runtime but the schema rejects
them as unexpected properties.

 Fix could be with a discriminated union, making the model reason about nested conditional types. However my concern is that lower-reasoning local LLMs will not manage this reasoning adequately.

Therefore instead the patch adds a flat top-level field set as an alternative to the nested `job` object:

| Field           | Type    | Notes                                          |
| --------------- | ------- | ---------------------------------------------- |
| `name`          | string  | job name                                       |
| `enabled`       | boolean |                                                |
| `sessionTarget` | string  | `main \| isolated \| current \| session:<id>`  |
| `at`            | string  | ISO-8601, one-shot                             |
| `everyMs`       | integer | recurring interval in ms                       |
| `expr`          | string  | cron expression                                |
| `tz`            | string  | IANA timezone for `expr`                       |
| `message`       | string  | agentTurn prompt — implies `agentTurn` payload |
| `anchorMs`      | integer | start anchor for `everyMs` schedules           |
| `model`         | string  | agentTurn model override                       |
| `fallbacks`     | string[]| agentTurn fallback models                      |
| `toolsAllow`    | string[]| agentTurn allowed tool ids                     |
| `thinking`      | string  | agentTurn thinking override                    |
| `timeoutSeconds`| number  | agentTurn timeout override                     |

The canonicalizer (`cron-tool-canonicalize.ts`) infers `schedule.kind` and `payload.kind` from whichever fields are present, so the model never has to deal with the nested union. Also
descriptions for `text` (implies `systemEvent`) and `mode` (wake only, not delivery mode)
have been tightened up.

The implementation now also normalises scalar toolsAllow / fallbacks into arrays and reconstructs job / patch from recoverable flat fields when the request is clearly a cron add/update call.  This deals with an issue found in testing where openclaw incorrectly tries toolsAllow as a scalar string, or emits a malformed non-object job / patch alongside flat fields.  

## User Impact

Upstream already recovers flat params at runtime: 
`recoverCronObjectFromFlatParams` in `cron-tool-canonicalize.ts` (with
`CRON_FLAT_PAYLOAD_KEYS` / `CRON_FLAT_SCHEDULE_KEYS`)

The patch simply exposes in the model-facing schema what the runtime already accepts but doesn't show. Note the nested `job` form still works also.  There is no negative user impact to the fix.

## Evidence

Tested on OpenClaw gateway version 2026.6.10
OS: Linux Mint 22.3 (Zena)
Kernel: Linux 6.17.0-23-generic (x86_64)
LLMs used for testing: Local Gemma 4 26b Q5 (llama.cpp); openai/gpt-5.4
Tests designed and run by: Claude (Anthropic Claude Opus 4.8); OpenAI Codex (gpt-5.4)

Focused validation on `fix/cron-tool-schema-fix` (`~/code/openclaw-main`) currently passes for the actual patch surface:

- `corepack pnpm test src/agents/tools/cron-tool.schema.test.ts src/agents/tools/cron-tool.test.ts`
  - schema shard: `20 passed`
  - agents shard: `135 passed`

These tests cover:

- flat-field exposure in the model-facing cron schema
- canonicalization of malformed flat `toolsAllow` / `fallbacks`
- synthesis of `job` / `patch` from recoverable flat arguments
- the drift guard that ensures any advertised flat field is still recoverable by the canonicalizer

Earlier branch testing also included:

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/tools/cron-tool.schema.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/tools/cron-tool.test.ts`
- `pnpm tsgo:core:test`

Live installed testing on patched OpenClaw 2026.6.10 also succeeded in creating disposable cron jobs through the flat-field interface, confirming the path works in a real installed gateway and not only in unit tests.

This four-file branch is green on the patch-local validation that actually exercises the cron-tool
change. The remaining PR-guide blockers are upstream baseline failures outside this patch scope.

Current upstream-baseline validation also shows:

- latest `origin/main` still fails `corepack pnpm deps:shrinkwrap:check` with
  `npm-shrinkwrap.json is stale. Run pnpm deps:shrinkwrap:generate.`
- clean `origin/main` is also currently red in unrelated test areas, including
  `src/gateway/server-methods/agent.test.ts` and `src/media-understanding/image.test.ts`
  (reported for completeness only)

So the branch is green for the cron-tool patch itself, while the remaining `check` / full-suite
`test` reds are upstream, pre-existing, and unrelated to this four-file change. codex review --base origin/main stated the patch appears to correctly expose the flat cron fields and normalize them before validation without introducing a concrete regression in the touched surfaces.

target.title: fix(tui): suppress duplicate assistant message after sessions.changed event
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves
handleSessionsChangedEvent in src/tui/tui-event-handlers.ts triggers loadHistory() when a sessions.changed event with reason: "new" fires. loadHistory() clears the chat log and replays all messages as static history entries. However, if a chat run was actively streaming at the time the event fired, the streaming final event can arrive after the history replay has already rendered the same message as a static entry. handleChatEvent unconditionally calls finalizeAssistant, which appends the message a second time — producing a duplicate in the chat log.

The race is:

User sends a message; the agent starts streaming (activeChatRunId / sessionRuns populated)
A sessions.changed "new" event fires (e.g. session file created/recreated on disk)
handleSessionsChangedEvent calls clearTrackedRunState() and queues loadHistory()
loadHistory() clears the chat log, replays the in-flight message as a static entry, removing the streaming component
The original streaming run's final event arrives → finalizeAssistant appends a second copy of the message
The same duplicate pattern could also affect delta and aborted/error states arriving late.

## Why This Change Was Made
The fix introduces a surrenderedToHistoryRunIds set that tracks run IDs handed off to a pending loadHistory() call. When handleSessionsChangedEvent receives a "new" event, it captures every tracked run ID (activeChatRunId, pendingChatRunId, sessionRuns, finalizedRuns) into this set. handleChatEvent then checks this set before processing chat events:

delta: suppressed entirely — the history replay already rendered the message as static
final: tracking state is finalized (activity indicator, watchdog, pending-run cleanup), but finalizeAssistant is not called — avoiding the duplicate render
aborted/error: cleaned up without appending duplicate history entries
A critical edge case is when loadHistory() restores a run as an in-flight streaming component (via inFlightRun). In that case, chatLog.hasStreamingRun() returns true, and the handler falls through to normal processing — the streaming component needs live delta/final events to reach its final state. The hasStreamingRun method was added to ChatLog as a clean query for this check.

A 15-second cleanup timer (unref'd so it doesn't keep the process alive) prevents the set from leaking run IDs indefinitely.

## User Impact
TUI users no longer see duplicate messages when a sessions.changed event fires mid-streaming. Normal chat flows are unaffected.

## Evidence
No new test file was added for this fix. The existing test suite in src/tui/tui-event-handlers.test.ts was extended to stub hasStreamingRun on the mock chat log.

What was not tested: live TUI sessions with real sessions.changed events during streaming.

target.title: docs: deprecate legacy session file helpers
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Related: #88838

Resolves a documentation and SDK-signaling gap where plugin authors could still see legacy session-store and transcript-file helpers as normal compatibility APIs even though the SQLite storage flip is preparing to remove those file-shaped surfaces.

## Why This Change Was Made

This marks the legacy whole-store, transcript-file, legacy transcript target, and low-level transcript append/update helper paths as deprecated on current main before the SQLite migration removes them. The replacement path is the scoped session entry helpers plus transcript identity helpers; this PR does not remove behavior or add a compatibility bridge.

## User Impact

Plugin developers get clearer migration guidance before the storage flip lands. Existing plugins continue to work on this branch, but new code is directed away from `sessions.json`, active JSONL transcript paths, and low-level transcript file emitters.

## Evidence

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/plugin-sdk/session-store-runtime.test.ts src/plugin-sdk/session-transcript-runtime.test.ts src/plugins/runtime/index.test.ts` -> passed, 45 tests.

- `git diff --check` -> passed.

target.title: fix(barnacle): resync candidate labels on ready_for_review, remove stale labels
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Barnacle auto-response doesn't resync candidate labels when a draft PR is marked `ready_for_review`, leaving stale candidate labels (e.g. `triage: blank-template`) on PRs with meaningful bodies.
- Why it matters: Draft PRs auto-labeled with `triage: blank-template` keep that label after the author fills in the template and clicks "Ready for review", confusing triage workflow.
- What changed: Added `ready_for_review` to the auto-response workflow trigger and script allowlist; rewrote `applyPullRequestCandidateLabels` to compute and remove stale candidate labels while preserving suppression/privileged-author guards.
- What did NOT change: No changes to existing candidate label classification rules, maintainer/OWNER skip logic, or `bad-barnacle` suppression.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] CI/CD / infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #77343
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: Three gaps — (1) `.github/workflows/auto-response.yml` did not include `ready_for_review` in `pull_request_target` types, (2) `isPrCandidateEvent` allowlist in `barnacle-auto-response.mjs` did not include `ready_for_review`, and (3) `applyPullRequestCandidateLabels` was add-only (never removed stale labels).
- Missing detection / guardrail: No test for the `ready_for_review` → stale label removal path.
- Contributing context (if known): N/A

## Real behavior proof

Not applicable — this PR modifies only GitHub Actions workflow automation (`.github/workflows/auto-response.yml`).
No product code, CLI, gateway, plugin, or UI surface is touched.
Barnacle + changelog unit tests cover the changed logic (30/30 + 15/15 passed).

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
- Target test or file: `test/scripts/barnacle-auto-response.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: `ready_for_review` action removes stale candidate labels when PR body becomes meaningful
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: Directly exercises `applyPullRequestCandidateLabels` return value + `removeLabels` call path
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): None — this was a new coverage gap
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

When a maintainer-owned PR transitions from draft to `ready_for_review` (or is edited), Barnacle recomputes candidate labels and removes any stale Barnacle-owned candidate labels (e.g. `triage: blank-template`) that no longer match the PR body/files.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[PR ready_for_review] -> [add missing labels only] -> [stale labels persist]

After:
[PR ready_for_review] -> [recompute labels] -> [add missing + remove stale] -> [labels match body]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No (existing GitHub API calls)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: any (GitHub Actions)
- Runtime/container: Node 24 (GitHub Actions)
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel: GitHub auto-response workflow

### Steps

1. Open a draft PR with a blank issue template body
2. Barnacle auto-labels it with `triage: blank-template`
3. Edit body to fill in the template (Problem/Why/What)
4. Click "Ready for review"

### Expected

- `triage: blank-template` is removed; correct candidate labels applied

### Actual

- `triage: blank-template` persisted (before fix)

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: `ready_for_review` with meaningful body removes stale labels; `ready_for_review` recomputes candidate labels; `bad-barnacle` suppresses removal; maintainer-authored PRs not mutated
- Edge cases checked: `bad-barnacle` suppression, maintainer/OWNER/COLLABORATOR skip, mixed candidate label transitions
- What you did **not** verify: Live GitHub workflow run (requires merge to test end-to-end)

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes`)
- Config/env changes? (`No`)
- Migration needed? (`No`)

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Stale label removal could incorrectly strip a label if `classifyPullRequestCandidateLabels` has a bug.
  - Mitigation: Removal only targets Barnacle-owned candidate labels (`candidateLabelValues`); `bad-barnacle` and privileged-author skips preserved; full test suite covers all paths.

target.title: feat(plugins): add before_model_call preflight hook
target.body:
## Summary

- Adds a typed, fail-closed `before_model_call` hook at Pi, CLI, and Codex app-server model submission boundaries.
- Gates non-bundled plugin conversation access behind `plugins.entries.<id>.hooks.allowConversationAccess`.
- Aligns CLI preflight and `llm_input` with the final prompt after retry selection, transformations, and image shaping, with a 15s default timeout.

## Changes

- `src/plugins/*` — hook contract, runner default timeout/fail-closed semantics, conversation gating compatibility record.
- `src/agents/*` — shared harness hook helper, Pi/CLI wiring, session-expiry retry coverage, and model-auth fixture compatibility with current `@mariozechner/pi-ai` types.
- `extensions/codex/src/app-server/*` — Codex preflight before `turn/start`.
- `docs/*`, generated config/API baselines, and changelog updated for the new hook.

## Testing

- Formal validation iter6: PASS at `9a94ed03f53b118b62ec6cdff293937637162df5`.

target.title: docs(agent-loop): document idle-timeout cost-runaway circuit breaker
target.body:
## What

Adds a bullet to the Timeouts section of `docs/concepts/agent-loop.md` documenting the idle-timeout circuit breaker introduced in #76345.

## Why

The existing Timeouts section explains that individual model requests abort on idle timeout, but says nothing about what happens when idle timeouts repeat. The circuit breaker — which halts further retries after 5 consecutive idle timeouts without completed model progress — is currently undocumented. Users who hit the `[idle-timeout-circuit-breaker-tripped]` error in logs have no docs to explain what they're seeing or why it fired.

## Accuracy

All claims in the added bullet are sourced directly from `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/idle-timeout-breaker.ts` and the CHANGELOG entry for #76345:
- Cap of 5: `MAX_CONSECUTIVE_IDLE_TIMEOUTS_BEFORE_OUTPUT = 5`
- Reset condition: `completedModelProgress` (completed text or tool-call progress), not partial output tokens
- Scope: fires across auth-profile retries inside the same embedded run
- Incident numbers: from the original issue #76293 report

target.title: diag(cli-runner): attribute AbortError trigger source
target.body:
## Problem

`[agent/cli-backend] claude live session turn failed: ... error=AbortError` log lines have no detail field, so operators cannot tell which of four distinct trigger paths fired:

1. User-typed abort word (`stop`, `wait`, etc. via `ABORT_TRIGGERS`)
2. `abortForRestart` from compaction-restart / drain
3. Mid-turn `/model` switch (`requestLiveSessionModelSwitch`)
4. Explicit `chat.abort` RPC

This makes the bug pattern impossible to fix without speculation. We've been seeing 20+ AbortError lines / 10 days across our deployment with no way to attribute them.

## Change

Diagnostic enrichment only — no behavior change.

- `src/agents/cli-runner/claude-live-session.ts`:
  - `createAbortError(reason?)` now accepts and includes the upstream abort reason in the error message (was: zero-arg, fixed `"CLI run aborted"`).
  - `failTurn` appends `detail="..."` (truncated to 240 chars) to the log line.
  - The two abort paths split: `abortFromSignal` reads `params.context.params.abortSignal.reason` (preserves `controller.abort(reason)` payload — `UserAbortError` for stop-words, `Error("Reply operation aborted for restart")` for compaction, etc.); `abortFromReplyBackend` tags `replyBackend:<reason>` (`user_abort`, `restart`, or `superseded`).
- `src/agents/cli-runner/execute.ts`: same `createCliAbortError(reason?)` upgrade for the non-live CLI path.
- `src/gateway/chat-abort.ts`: `abortChatRunById` calls `controller.abort("chat-abort:<stopReason>")` instead of `.abort()`, so the existing `stopReason` (e.g., `"rpc"`, `"stop"`, `"timeout"`) propagates.

## Effect on log lines

Before:
\`\`\`
... error=AbortError
\`\`\`

After (one of):
\`\`\`
... error=AbortError detail="CLI run aborted: AbortError: Reply operation aborted by user"
... error=AbortError detail="CLI run aborted: AbortError: Reply operation aborted for restart"
... error=AbortError detail="CLI run aborted: replyBackend:restart"
... error=AbortError detail="CLI run aborted: chat-abort:rpc"
\`\`\`

That is sufficient to attribute each abort to its real source (operator stop-word vs. compaction restart vs. model switch vs. RPC) without re-investigating from scratch.

## Tests

- New: `src/agents/cli-runner/abort-reason.test.ts` — 7 unit tests covering string / Error / object / nullish abort-reason inputs for both `createAbortError` (live-session path) and `createCliAbortError` (non-live path). All pass.
- Existing: `src/gateway/chat-abort.test.ts` 4/4 pass; `src/agents/cli-runner/` 35/35 pass.
- `pnpm tsgo -p tsconfig.core.json` and `pnpm tsgo -p tsconfig.core.test.json` both clean.

`pnpm check:changed` not run — would be appreciated as part of CI verification.

## Out of scope

I deliberately did NOT raise the abort grace timeout, change `ABORT_TRIGGERS`, or alter compaction-restart policy. Without trigger evidence those would be guesses. This PR is the prerequisite — once a few new aborts arrive with attributed reasons, the actual fix becomes obvious.

## Provenance

This change was scoped and authored by a Claude Code background agent during a source-level diagnostic of multiple gateway issues. Full investigation writeup (root causes, failing call paths, what wasn't fixed and why) is in `DIAGNOSTIC.md` on the branch but not included in the diff so it doesn't pollute the repo.

target.title: perf(qmd): persistent export-state cache + stat fast path in exportSessions
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** For agents with thousands of session transcripts, `QmdMemoryManager.exportSessions()` was the dominant gateway CPU consumer. Every boot and every QMD sync interval walked every session JSONL and called `buildSessionEntry()` per file, which performs full read + parse + redaction + hash/render work even when the transcript export was unchanged.
- **Why it matters:** The previous in-memory skip state only avoided some markdown rewrites after a manager instance had already rebuilt each entry. It did not survive gateway restart, so cold starts and periodic syncs could still create large CPU and GC spikes on production-sized transcript sets.
- **What changed:**
  1. Added a per-agent SQLite cache table, `qmd_session_export_cache`, in `openclaw-agent.sqlite`, keyed by `(session_file, export_dir, render_version)`.
  2. Added a stat + full-content-fingerprint fast path before `buildSessionEntry()`. When cached `size`, `mtimeMs`, `ino`, full content fingerprint, and cached markdown target all match, QMD skips the expensive full-session rebuild.
  3. Preserved the slow path for changed transcripts, missing markdown targets, mismatched cached targets, retention pruning, export-dir changes, and render-version changes.
  4. Kept raw per-agent DB/Kysely access inside the core state owner and exposed only QMD-specific cache helpers through a private local-only SDK subpath, `openclaw/plugin-sdk/memory-core-qmd-export-cache-internal`, scoped to the bundled `memory-core` plugin.
  5. Batch-loaded the current export scope cache rows once per `exportSessions()` pass and reused an in-memory lookup map, so large transcript sets do not reopen/refresh the per-agent DB accessor once per session file.
  6. Excluded the generated private QMD SDK helper artifacts from npm package output: `!dist/plugin-sdk/memory-core-qmd-export-cache-internal.js` and `!dist/plugin-sdk/memory-core-qmd-export-cache-internal.d.ts`.

**Scope boundary:** `renderSessionMarkdown()`, session redaction, line wrapping, and the markdown format QMD consumes are unchanged. `buildSessionEntry()` remains the authoritative slow path. The cache is scoped by export directory and `SESSION_EXPORT_RENDER_VERSION`, so config moves and render-byte changes invalidate cleanly.

**Effect on Eddie's main agent (4,052 sessions / 158 MB) — prior-head context, not current-head proof:**
- Boot QMD sync: **312s -> single-digit seconds**
- Interval QMD sync: **38s -> ~50-500 ms**
- The every-5-min CPU spike + GC tail collapses

> ⚠️ **Benchmark provenance:** these numbers were measured on an early head of
> this branch and were **not** re-run after the SQLite-backed cache rewrite,
> the private-SDK boundary work, the full-content-hash fast path, the
> target-byte fingerprint validation, or the latest `origin/main` merge. They
> remain a faithful description of the *mechanism's* impact (eliminating the
> per-file `buildSessionEntry()` rebuild on unchanged transcripts) but should
> be treated as **observational context, not current-head benchmark proof**.
> Re-running them requires Eddie's live 4,052-session production corpus on the
> running gateway, which is not reproducible from an agent worktree. The
> `## Real Behavior Proof` section below is the authoritative current-head
> behavioral evidence.

## Linked Issue

- Closes openclaw-ys0 (Beads tracking issue)

## Test Coverage

New and updated tests cover the cache contract:

- `extensions/memory-core/src/memory/qmd-manager.test.ts`
  - persists session-export state across manager restarts
  - rebuilds after same-size transcript rewrites, proving content changes are not hidden by matching stat fields
  - ignores cache rows scoped to a previous `exportDir`
  - rebuilds markdown when the cached target has been deleted
  - rebuilds the expected markdown when a current-scope cache row points at another target
  - preserves existing session-export behavior for checkpoint/session filtering
- `src/state/openclaw-agent-db.test.ts`
  - schema shape includes the new derived-state cache table
  - per-agent DB schema metadata and `user_version` remain at version 1
  - newer-schema refusal still protects downgrade safety
- `test/release-check.test.ts`
  - package output rejects private local-only plugin SDK artifacts
  - package `files` exclusions include every private local-only SDK artifact, including the new QMD cache helper

## Tradeoffs / Known Caveats

- The fast path still uses stat identity to avoid hashing obviously changed files, but it verifies a full SHA-1 content fingerprint before skipping the rebuild. Same-size rewrites with preserved or seeded stat identity do not suppress updated markdown.
- `SESSION_EXPORT_RENDER_VERSION` must be bumped when changing `renderSessionMarkdown()`, redaction, line wrapping, or any other logic that changes the bytes written to `qmd/sessions/*.md`.
- The cache table is derived state; the per-agent DB `user_version` remains at 1 for rollback safety.
- No hand-written `CHANGELOG.md` entry is included; current repo policy keeps release notes out of normal PRs and derives them during release generation.

## Real Behavior Proof

- **Behavior addressed:** QMD session export persists its unchanged-transcript fast-path state in the per-agent SQLite DB without exposing raw per-agent DB/Kysely access through the public plugin SDK. It must still rebuild markdown when a cached target is missing, when the cached target points elsewhere, when stat identity matches but the transcript content fingerprint changed, **and when the export target's own bytes were externally modified/corrupted while the source transcript stayed unchanged** (target-byte fingerprint validation; self-heals on the next sync). The private QMD SDK artifact must also stay out of package output.

- **Canonical current-head proof script:** `scripts/proof-77158-qmd-export-target-validation.ts` (run with `pnpm tsx scripts/proof-77158-qmd-export-target-validation.ts`). It drives the **real private `QmdMemoryManager.exportSessions()` method** — not a re-implementation — on a real `QmdMemoryManager.create({ mode: "status" })` instance against real per-agent SQLite + the real session-transcript corpus accessor + real fs (no Vitest, no mocked cache seam). `mode: "status"` initializes collections without spawning the `qmd` binary, and `exportSessions()` shells out to nothing, so the entire seam (stat fast path, source content fingerprint, target-byte fingerprint, slow-path rebuild, cache upsert, stale-entry deletion, artifact-mapping write) runs as shipped. Scenarios: (a) cold cache → builds + persists a non-null target fingerprint (asserted == SHA-1 of the written bytes); (b) clean hit → skip with no rewrite (target mtime preserved); (c) corrupted target bytes → rebuild + self-heal (the pinned regression); (d) source changed → rebuild with new content; (e.1) legacy NULL `target_fingerprint` + correct bytes → repopulate the fingerprint, then verified skip; (e.2) legacy NULL + corrupted target → rebuild. Output ends `All runtime assertions passed.` Focused vitest regressions in `qmd-manager.test.ts` pin the same paths in the unit suite.
- **Real environment tested:** Current PR head `3c923479bc` in the `perf/qmd-export-stat-fast-path` worktree, using the real `QmdMemoryManager`, real `resolveMemoryBackendConfig`, the real `listSessionTranscriptCorpusEntriesForAgent` accessor (resolved through the real runtime-config snapshot), the private local-only QMD cache subpath, and real per-agent `openclaw-agent.sqlite` storage. The proof ran against an isolated `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR` under `/tmp`; no Vitest harness or mocked QMD export cache was used, and the `qmd` binary was never invoked (it is not on the export path).
- **Exact steps run:** `pnpm tsx scripts/proof-77158-qmd-export-target-validation.ts` from the topic worktree. The script seeds one session JSONL + a `sessions.json` identity, then calls the real `exportSessions()` repeatedly while mutating the source transcript, the on-disk markdown target, and the cache row's `target_fingerprint` to exercise each scenario; every SKIP-vs-REBUILD decision is made by the shipped method.
- **Evidence after fix:** Captured terminal output from the live run (also saved under `~/reports/proof-77158/run-output.txt`):

```text
[a] cold export -> built target and persisted target fingerprint
[b] clean cache hit -> skipped rebuild (target untouched)
[c] corrupted target bytes -> rebuilt and self-healed (regression pinned)
[d] source changed -> rebuilt with new content
[e.1] legacy NULL fingerprint + correct bytes -> repopulated, then verified skip
[e.2] legacy NULL fingerprint + corrupted target -> rebuilt (corruption healed)
All runtime assertions passed.
```

- **Observed result after fix:** (a) the cold export built the markdown and persisted a non-null target fingerprint equal to the SHA-1 of the written bytes; (b) the clean hit left the target's mtime and content untouched, proving the verified fast path; (c) a corrupted target was rebuilt and self-healed, with the next round skipping again; (d) a changed source rebuilt with the new content; (e.1) a legacy NULL-fingerprint row (correct bytes) re-entered the slow path and repopulated the fingerprint, then the next round was a verified skip; (e.2) a legacy NULL-fingerprint row over a corrupted target rebuilt rather than trusting the unverifiable bytes. Final cache state tracks exactly the one exported session.
- **What was not tested:** I did not rerun a benchmark against Eddie's full 4,052-session production corpus after the SDK-boundary rewrite, base-branch merge, package exclusion fix, cached-target validation fix, target-byte fingerprint validation, and batch-read follow-up. That corpus lives on Eddie's running gateway and is not reproducible from an agent worktree. The proof above (and `scripts/proof-77158-qmd-export-target-validation.ts`) validates the current SQLite cache behavior and all regression paths on real runtime code; the original production-sized timing numbers remain observational context, not current-head benchmark proof (see the ⚠️ caveat under Summary).

## Verification

Current head `3c923479bc` includes:

- `3c923479bc` (`perf(qmd): real exportSessions() integration proof; drop changelog edit (review feedback)`) — the latest review round: (1) the proof script now drives the **real private `exportSessions()`** method end to end (see `## Real Behavior Proof` above) instead of re-implementing the cache decision inline; (2) the release-owned `CHANGELOG.md` `## Unreleased` block is removed (restored byte-identical to `main`).
- `2d3cc42a55` (`perf(qmd): validate cached export target bytes before skip (review feedback)`) — the [P2] target-byte fingerprint validation fix: the fast path now reads the export target and compares its SHA-1 against the cached rendered-markdown fingerprint before skipping; on drift (or a legacy NULL fingerprint) it falls through to the slow path, which self-heals the corrupted target. Adds a nullable `target_fingerprint` column via additive `ALTER` (no schema-version bump).
- `33a9ac2566` (`chore(qmd): refresh plugin SDK API baseline after main merge (review feedback)`) — regenerated `docs/.generated/plugin-sdk-api-baseline.sha256` (tracked file only; `.json`/`.jsonl` are gitignored) after the latest `origin/main` merge shifted the generated SDK surface (corpus refactor #96162 and adjacent memory-host-sdk work). Deterministic across repeated `pnpm plugin-sdk:api:gen` runs.
- `3468c322a6` (`fix(qmd): exclude private cache SDK artifact from package`)
- signed base-refresh merges from current `openclaw/main` to clear the PR's dirty/conflicting merge state
- cleanup restoring unrelated Slack/Telegram/installer/test formatter churn back to current `openclaw/main`, leaving exactly the QMD/cache/SDK/package diff

**Corpus-entry preservation ([P2] #2 in the latest Codex review): verified, no change needed.** Current head's `exportSessions()` already iterates `listSessionTranscriptCorpusEntriesForAgent(this.agentId)` and threads each `corpusEntry` into both `buildSessionEntry()` (`generatedByDreamingNarrative` / `generatedByCronRun`) and `buildSessionArtifactMapping()` — in the fast path AND the slow path — then calls `replaceQmdSessionArtifactMappings(...)`. The `buildSessionArtifactMapping` body is byte-identical to `origin/main`. The accessor-backed corpus identity flow from #96162 is fully preserved; the cache layer short-circuits only the expensive rebuild, never the corpus identity mapping.

**Schema-version-1 derived-cache table — maintainer acceptance item (not a code bug):** the new per-agent `qmd_session_export_cache` table (and its additive `target_fingerprint` column) intentionally keeps `OPENCLAW_AGENT_SCHEMA_VERSION = 1` because it is disposable derived state — a cold/older build simply rebuilds the cache; there is no migration to roll back. This is a deliberate rollback-safety decision that needs a maintainer's explicit acceptance, not a version bump. Flagged for maintainer judgment rather than changed unilaterally.

**Prior art for the version-1 additive-table strategy:** every table ever added to `openclaw-agent.sqlite` has followed the same convention — `CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS` at `user_version = 1`, no version bump, no migration required. The full history on `origin/main`:

| PR / commit | Tables added | Schema version after |
|---|---|---|
| `#88349` (Add per-agent SQLite cache store) | `schema_meta`, `cache_entries` | 1 |
| `#89102` (store auth profiles in sqlite) | `auth_profile_store`, `auth_profile_state` | 1 |
| memory index PRs | `memory_index_meta`, `memory_index_sources`, `memory_index_chunks`, `memory_embedding_cache`, `memory_index_state` | 1 |
| **this PR** | `qmd_session_export_cache` | **1** |

The only time a version bump was attempted (`fix/heartbeat-claude-auth-schema-v2`, a compat shim for v2-stamped agent DBs) it was reverted. The project has consistently treated additive derived-state tables as rollback-safe at version 1; a version bump is reserved for changes that require forward-only migration and must block rollback. `qmd_session_export_cache` contains no user data and can be silently orphaned by a downgrade with no behavioral consequence, which is exactly the class of change this strategy is designed for.

**Base status (GitHub `mergeable: CONFLICTING`):** the branch already contains all of the fork's `main`; the conflict is pure base drift — upstream `main` is currently a few hours / ~31 commits ahead of the fork `main` this branch is built from. A trial merge of current upstream `main` produced exactly one auto-resolvable conflict (the generated `docs/.generated/plugin-sdk-api-baseline.sha256`), with `qmd-manager.ts`, its test, and `package.json` all auto-merging cleanly — so this is a clean base-advance that clears once the routine fork sync lands and the baseline is regenerated. No substantive code conflict exists.

Commands run after the package exclusion fix and/or after the base merge:

```text
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs test/release-check.test.ts
# 56 passed

node scripts/check-plugin-sdk-subpath-exports.mjs
# OK: all referenced openclaw/plugin-sdk/<subpath> imports are exported

node scripts/check-extension-plugin-sdk-boundary.mjs --mode=src-outside-plugin-sdk
# no violations; baseline matches

node scripts/check-extension-plugin-sdk-boundary.mjs --mode=plugin-sdk-internal
# no violations; baseline matches

pnpm plugin-sdk:api:check
# OK docs/.generated/plugin-sdk-api-baseline.sha256

pnpm plugin-sdk:surface:check
# all SDK entrypoints: 339; public package SDK entrypoints: 321; local-only entrypoints: 18; package-exported forbidden subpaths: 0

pnpm exec oxfmt --check package.json docs/.generated/plugin-sdk-api-baseline.sha256 extensions/memory-core/src/memory/qmd-manager.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory/qmd-manager.test.ts src/plugin-sdk/memory-core-qmd-export-cache-internal.ts src/plugins/sdk-alias.ts scripts/lib/plugin-sdk-private-local-only-subpaths.json
# clean

pnpm tsgo:core
# exited 0

pnpm tsgo:core:test
# exited 0

git diff --check
# clean

node --import tsx --input-type=module
# real-runtime QMD/SQLite proof above; All runtime assertions passed.
```

target.title: feat(tools/write): add append mode for agent writes
target.body:
## Summary

Revives and rebases the closed append-mode work from #75549 for the canonical data-loss issue #40001.

The `write` tool currently overwrites existing files. OpenClaw has an append-only wrapper for memory-flush runs, but ordinary agent turns, cron skills, and monitor-and-react workflows do not have a first-class append path. This PR adds a backwards-compatible optional `append: true` parameter to `write` so agents can append without synthesizing a read-concatenate-overwrite sequence.

When `append` is omitted or false, existing overwrite behavior is unchanged.

## What changed

- Adds optional `append?: boolean` handling for host/workspace and sandbox write operations.
- Preserves existing workspace/root guard behavior.
- Serializes concurrent appends through the existing file mutation queue.
- Preserves validation behavior for blank paths and invalid content.
- Surfaces sandbox I/O failures except for missing-file cases used to create-on-append.
- Honors aborted append calls before entering the mutation queue.
- Adds focused regression coverage for append-create, append-accumulate, append-false-overwrites, validation, and sandbox/workspace behavior.

## Validation

Run in an isolated checkout on current `origin/main`:

- `corepack pnpm install --frozen-lockfile`
- `corepack pnpm exec vitest run src/agents/pi-tools.workspace-only-false.test.ts src/agents/pi-tools.create-openclaw-coding-tools.test.ts src/agents/pi-tools.read.host-tilde-expansion.test.ts src/agents/pi-tools.sandbox-mounted-paths.workspace-only.test.ts src/agents/pi-tools.params.test.ts`
  - passed: `10 files / 170 tests`
- `git diff --check`
- `corepack pnpm lint:core`
  - passed: `0 warnings / 0 errors`

## Notes

This PR intentionally keeps the API small and backwards-compatible. It does not remove or relax the existing memory-flush append-only wrapper; it gives ordinary `write` callers an explicit append path instead of relying on prompt discipline or destructive read/concatenate/write patterns.

Fixes #40001.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed:** The agent write tool supports `append: true` without overwriting the existing file, while still using the workspace-root guard for workspace-only host writes.
- **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw source checkout on Linux using the actual `createHostWorkspaceWriteTool` implementation against a temporary workspace on the real filesystem.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran a `node --import tsx` terminal smoke that created the host workspace write tool, wrote `first line`, reran the same write tool with `append: true` for `second line`, then read the resulting file.
- **Evidence after fix:** Terminal capture from the smoke:

  ```text
  workspace: $TMPDIR/openclaw-write-append-proof-.../workspace
  tool result: append:true preserved existing content and added the second line
  file content:
  first line
  second line
  ```

- **Observed result after fix:** The final file retained the original content and appended the new content in order; the append path did not truncate or replace the file.
- **What was not tested:** No live Gateway session was exercised; validation used the real tool implementation in an isolated local checkout plus targeted regression/type checks.

target.body:
## Summary
- add ACPX `codexHome` config plus `OPENCLAW_ACPX_CODEX_HOME` env override for the Codex ACP wrapper
- keep the existing isolated `CODEX_HOME` default when no override is configured
- preserve the newer Codex wrapper diagnostics, safe isolated config copying, process cleanup behavior, and API-key auth materialization from current `main`
- document the override in the ACP router skill and plugin manifest

## Latest update
- rebased onto current `main` at `4ecb45bf77`
- removed the normal-PR `CHANGELOG.md` entry
- added test isolation so host `CODEX_HOME` / `OPENCLAW_ACPX_CODEX_HOME` environment values do not make wrapper tests depend on the developer machine

## Validation
- `pnpm test extensions/acpx/src/config.test.ts extensions/acpx/src/codex-auth-bridge.test.ts` - 2 files / 36 tests passed
- `pnpm test:extension acpx` - 13 files / 145 tests passed
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 extensions/acpx/src/config-schema.ts extensions/acpx/src/config.ts extensions/acpx/src/codex-auth-bridge.ts extensions/acpx/src/codex-auth-bridge.test.ts extensions/acpx/src/config.test.ts extensions/acpx/openclaw.plugin.json extensions/acpx/skills/acp-router/SKILL.md`
- `pnpm check:test-types`
- `CI=1 PNPM_CONFIG_VERIFY_DEPS_BEFORE_RUN=false pnpm check:changed`

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: ACPX Codex auth now honors an explicit Codex home override instead of always forcing the generated isolated `CODEX_HOME`; this lets operators point the bundled Codex ACP bridge at a known auth/profile directory while keeping the isolated default path unchanged.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout on Linux with Node v22.22.0 and pnpm 11.2.2, repaired PR branch based on current upstream main. Secrets, user identifiers, private paths, and deployment-specific names omitted.
- Default isolated path check:

```text
isolated_default {"codexHomeMode":"wrapper-local","configExists":true,"command":"\"/usr/bin/node\" \"<wrapper>/codex-acp-wrapper.mjs\""}
```

- Explicit `codexHome` path check:

```text
explicit_override {"handleBackend":"acpx","runtimeSessionName":"present","events":["status","error","message:Internal error"],"text":"available commands updated (6)","codexHome":"<home>/.codex"}
```

- Observed result after fix: the repaired branch preserves the default wrapper-local isolated Codex home and the explicit override path reaches an OpenClaw-managed ACPX Codex runtime handle with `CODEX_HOME=<home>/.codex`.
- What still blocked full live success proof: the host Codex OAuth state was not fresh during the final live run. The wrapper stderr showed redacted Codex auth errors `refresh_token_reused` / `token_expired`, so a successful authenticated Codex response still needs to be rerun after refreshing host Codex login. No token or private path is included here.

target.title: fix(cli): exit after hooks inspection output
target.body:
## Summary

- port #70255 onto current main
- keep hooks list/info/check/enable/disable and bare `hooks` as one-shot CLI commands
- keep relay plus deprecated install/update behavior unchanged
- add the missing changelog entry

## Why

Plugin diagnostics can load third-party hook plugins. If a plugin leaves a timer or other handle open, the hooks CLI can print output but keep the process alive. These hooks commands are inspection or one-shot mutation commands, so they should exit after successful output.

Supersedes #70255.

## Tests

- `pnpm test src/cli/hooks-cli.test.ts src/plugins/status.test.ts`
- `pnpm check:no-conflict-markers`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 CHANGELOG.md src/cli/hooks-cli.ts src/cli/hooks-cli.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: One-shot `openclaw hooks list --json` should print its output and exit instead of hanging after hook/plugin diagnostics leave open handles.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw source checkout on macOS, using this PR branch and the real CLI path, not a unit test.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran the actual CLI command with a 20 second watchdog from the PR branch: `pnpm openclaw hooks list --json`.
- Evidence after fix: Terminal output from the real CLI command:

```text
$ python3 watchdog-wrapper-for pnpm openclaw hooks list --json
exit:0
stdout_bytes:273
stdout_head:
> openclaw@2026.5.4 openclaw /Users/doruk/Desktop/Asuman/.worktrees/openclaw-hooks-cli-exit-refresh
> node scripts/run-node.mjs hooks list --json

{
  "workspaceDir": "/Users/doruk/.openclaw/workspace",
  "managedHooksDir": "/Users/doruk/.openclaw/hooks",
  "hooks": []
}
```

- Observed result after fix: The command exited with status 0 before the 20 second watchdog fired and returned the hooks JSON output.
- What was not tested: No separate hanging third-party plugin was installed for this proof. The focused regression test still covers the lingering-handle path.

target.title: fix(discord): preserve target intent during normalization
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Discord-like explicit DM targets could be normalized through the id-like target path without preserving the intended direct-message kind.
- Why it matters: A Discord target that is syntactically a user/direct target should stay routed as a user/direct target instead of falling back to generic group/channel inference.
- What changed: Shared target-kind inference now lives in `target-normalization`, and both outbound target resolution and cron delivery pass the inferred kind into id-like plugin resolution.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): No Discord runtime sending behavior, config schema, plugin manifest, or user-facing docs changed.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [x] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes N/A
- Related N/A
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: Explicit Discord direct-message targets such as `user:<id>` preserve inferred user/direct intent when id-like target resolution runs for outbound and cron delivery.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout on Windows/PowerShell, Discord plugin configured with a real bot token and a redacted explicit `user:<id>` target.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran local OpenClaw Discord outbound/cron delivery paths against an explicit `user:<id>` Discord target using the patched branch, then compared the live delivery result with the new resolver/cron coverage.
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output): Existing live-result report from @AScriver after the local Discord run, with private IDs and tokens redacted:

```text
Scenario: Discord outbound/cron delivery to an explicit user:<id> target
Result: delivered to the intended DM recipient and preserved explicit user target intent instead of falling back to generic group/channel inference
Coverage alignment: matches the targeted resolver and cron tests added in this PR
```

- Observed result after fix: Discord delivered to the intended DM recipient, and the explicit user/direct target intent was preserved instead of falling back to generic group/channel inference.
- What was not tested: Raw Discord token and private IDs are not published; no additional non-Discord channels were tested live.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): Source-level before-state showed the id-like resolver did not receive inferred `preferredKind` for outbound/cron paths; the targeted regression tests cover that missing handoff.
## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: id-like target resolution received only the raw target string, so the plugin resolver could lose the caller's inferred direct-message intent after normalization.
- Missing detection / guardrail: tests covered id-like fallback and directory inference separately, but not propagation of inferred DM/user kind into plugin id-like resolution.
- Contributing context (if known): Discord target parsing can distinguish user/direct targets before the generic id-like normalization path runs.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/infra/outbound/target-resolver.test.ts`, `src/cron/isolated-agent/delivery-target.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: inferred direct-message/user kind is passed into id-like plugin target resolution.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: it validates the shared resolver seam and cron delivery call path without needing a live Discord bot.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): N/A
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Discord-targeted delivery paths preserve explicit direct-message intent more reliably when resolving id-like targets. No config or command syntax changes.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
Discord DM-like target -> generic id-like resolution -> kind may default away from user/direct intent

After:
Discord DM-like target -> infer user/direct kind -> id-like resolution receives preferredKind=user -> direct target preserved
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`): No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`): No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Windows, PowerShell
- Runtime/container: local repo checkout, Node/pnpm from workspace
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): Discord target routing logic
- Relevant config (redacted): N/A

### Steps

1. Resolve a plugin id-like target whose channel messaging adapter infers `direct` chat type.
2. Resolve a cron delivery target for an explicit DM-like target.
3. Assert the id-like resolver receives `preferredKind: "user"` and preserves the returned target.

### Expected

- The id-like plugin resolver receives the inferred user/direct kind.
- Cron delivery preserves explicit DM intent after target normalization.

### Actual

- Verified by the new focused tests.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

Targeted validation:

```bash
pnpm test src/infra/outbound/target-resolver.test.ts src/cron/isolated-agent/delivery-target.test.ts
pnpm check:changed
```

## Human Verification (required)

What I personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: local Discord outbound/cron delivery to a redacted explicit `user:<id>` target was reported after this patch; target resolver and cron delivery target unit/seam coverage also passed locally.
- Edge cases checked: id-like raw targets continue passing `preferredKind: "group"`; explicit DM-like targets pass `preferredKind: "user"`.
- What I did **not** verify: raw Discord token/private IDs are intentionally not published; no additional non-Discord channels were tested live.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`): Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`): No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`): No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: shared target kind inference could affect non-Discord channels that expose `inferTargetChatType`.
  - Mitigation: the helper preserves existing fallback behavior, maps `channel` to `group` where directory lookup expects groups, and adds focused coverage for the new id-like propagation path.

target.title: fix(plugins): supplement ClawHub artifact metadata
target.body:
## Summary

- Accept current ClawHub artifact resolver responses where `artifact` is the package artifact summary shape.
- When the resolver response has a legacy zip artifact but no archive verification metadata, load the version metadata and use its `sha256hash` or `files[]` for verification.
- Add a regression test for the current `clawhub:zeroapi@3.8.9` response shape.

## Why

The ClawHub artifact endpoint can return a legacy zip artifact summary while the install verification metadata is only present on the version endpoint. OpenClaw rejected that package as missing verification metadata, even though the package was valid and installable.

## Tests

- `pnpm test src/plugins/clawhub.test.ts`
- `pnpm tsgo:core`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/infra/clawhub.ts src/plugins/clawhub.ts src/plugins/clawhub.test.ts && git diff --check`
- `pnpm check:changed`

## Real-world validation

- `HOME=$tmp/home OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=$tmp/state OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=$tmp/openclaw.json pnpm openclaw plugins install clawhub:zeroapi@3.8.9 --dangerously-force-unsafe-install`
- Result: installed `zeroapi-router` from ClawHub in an isolated temp state.

## AI-assisted

Yes. I used Codex and reviewed the changed code and tests.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: Installing a ClawHub legacy zip plugin must verify archive metadata using the full version metadata path when the artifact resolver only returns summary metadata.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw source checkout on macOS, using this PR branch and an isolated temporary `OPENCLAW_HOME`, installing the live `clawhub:zeroapi` package.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran the real CLI install path from the PR branch: `OPENCLAW_HOME=/tmp/openclaw-real-proof-NSAg8l pnpm openclaw plugins install clawhub:zeroapi --force`.
- Evidence after fix: Terminal output from the real CLI command:

```text

```

- Observed result after fix: The live ClawHub plugin install resolved version metadata, downloaded and extracted the plugin, and exited with status 0 in a fresh temporary OpenClaw home.
- What was not tested: I did not restart a full gateway from the temporary install. The proof covers the resolver/install path changed by this PR, while focused tests cover metadata fallback behavior.

target.title: fix: prune all history images when current turn has fresh attachments
target.body:
## Problem

When sending a new image for analysis, stale image data from previous conversation turns can contaminate the model's interpretation of the current image. Reported in #66702.

## Solution

- Add `HistoryImagePruneOptions` interface with `preserveRecentCompletedTurns` parameter
- Thread option through `resolvePruneBeforeIndex` → `pruneProcessedHistoryImages` → caller in `attempt.ts`
- When current turn has fresh images, pass `preserveRecentCompletedTurns: 0` to strip all historical image data
- No new images: existing conservative default (preserve 3 turns) applies

Backward-compatible. Existing tests unchanged.

Fixes #66702
